prototypes.name: vanguard-scroll
prototypes.mechanic: multi-directional scrolling with 4-way simultaneous fire
prototypes.ancestry: Vanguard (1981, TOSE, SNK), Scramble (1981, Konami)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move/aim | Space — energy shield | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 2 (Expert Systems — multi-directional threat assessment)
prototypes.aiInsight: Vanguard's 4-way fire combined with changing scroll directions forces the player to maintain situational awareness on all axes simultaneously — a multi-directional threat assessment problem that foreshadows sensor fusion in autonomous systems.
prototypes.notes: Changing scroll direction between zones keeps the player disoriented.

prototypes.name: text-adventure
prototypes.mechanic: parse text commands to explore rooms and solve puzzles
prototypes.ancestry: Zork (1977, Infocom), Colossal Cave Adventure (1976, Crowther/Woods)
prototypes.controls: Type commands + Enter — interact | R (at game over) — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 2 (Expert Systems — rule-based parsing and world state)
prototypes.aiInsight: Text adventures are the original natural language interfaces — Zork's parser was a rule-based expert system that mapped English verbs to world-state mutations, predating modern NLU by decades. The game world is a knowledge graph traversed by symbolic reasoning.
prototypes.notes: The text parser is the mechanic — the game is about language as interface.

prototypes.name: crown-adventure
prototypes.mechanic: explore rooms, collect items, solve inventory puzzles
prototypes.ancestry: King's Quest (1984, Sierra/Roberta Williams), Adventure (1980, Atari/Warren Robinett)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move | Space — interact/pick up | I — inventory | 1-5 — select item | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — adventure game logic, state machines)
prototypes.aiInsight: King's Quest transformed text adventure state machines into spatial puzzles — the dependency graph of items and locks became a physical world the player walks through. This is constraint satisfaction made tangible, where the search space is literal geography.
prototypes.notes: Inventory puzzles are dependency graphs in disguise.

prototypes.name: quest-explorer
prototypes.mechanic: explore top-down world, fight with sword, find items
prototypes.ancestry: The Legend of Zelda (1986, Nintendo/Shigeru Miyamoto), Hydlide (1984, T&E Soft)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move | Space — sword attack | Z — use item | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — open world design, player-driven exploration)
prototypes.aiInsight: Zelda's open world is a directed graph with soft gates — the player can theoretically go anywhere, but difficulty gradients and item requirements create an implicit curriculum. This is reward shaping through world design, decades before the concept was formalized in reinforcement learning.
prototypes.notes: The overworld is a curriculum disguised as geography.

prototypes.name: animated-quest
prototypes.mechanic: QTE cinematic adventure — timed directional reactions to scenes
prototypes.ancestry: Dragon's Lair (1983, Don Bluth/Rick Dyer, Advanced Microcomputer Systems), Space Ace (1984, Don Bluth)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — directional QTE | Space — action | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — pre-rendered cinematic interaction)
prototypes.aiInsight: Dragon's Lair reduced gameplay to a binary tree of success/failure at predetermined branch points — effectively a decision tree, the fundamental data structure of 1980s expert systems. The game IS a playable decision tree, making its connection to contemporary AI unmistakable.
prototypes.notes: QTE mechanic strips interaction to pure timing and reaction.

prototypes.name: haunted-puzzle
prototypes.mechanic: point-and-click adventure with verb interface and multiple characters
prototypes.ancestry: Maniac Mansion (1987, Ron Gilbert/Gary Winnick, Lucasfilm Games), Zork (1977, Infocom)
prototypes.controls: Mouse — click verbs/objects | 1/2/3 — switch character | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — SCUMM engine and state-machine puzzles)
prototypes.aiInsight: Maniac Mansion's SCUMM engine abstracted adventure game logic into a verb-noun state machine, creating one of the first domain-specific languages for interactive narrative. The verb-object-target grammar is structurally identical to the action-entity-parameter patterns in modern task-planning AI systems.
prototypes.notes: Multiple character abilities create parallel solution paths — proto-emergent design.

prototypes.name: globe-detective
prototypes.mechanic: geography detective — gather clues, travel between cities, issue warrant, catch thief
prototypes.ancestry: Where in the World Is Carmen Sandiego? (1985, Broderbund), Where in Time Is Carmen Sandiego? (1989, Broderbund)
prototypes.controls: 1-4 — menu choices | Mouse — click options | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — database-driven educational gameplay)
prototypes.aiInsight: Carmen Sandiego combined a geographic knowledge database with deductive reasoning gameplay — the player essentially performs a search algorithm through a graph of cities, using clue constraints to prune the suspect space. This is constraint satisfaction in game form.
prototypes.notes: Geography detective game. Travel between world cities gathering clues.

prototypes.name: border-check
prototypes.mechanic: document inspection — compare passports and permits against rules that change daily; approve or deny entry; balance income against morality
prototypes.ancestry: Papers, Please (2013, Lucas Pope / 3909 LLC)
prototypes.controls: Mouse — click documents, stamps, buttons | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 6 (Deep Learning Dawn — pattern matching, anomaly detection)
prototypes.aiInsight: Papers, Please is anomaly detection in human form. The player acts as a classifier — scanning documents for discrepancies against a rule set, exactly like a trained neural network detecting fraudulent inputs. Each day adds features to the decision boundary, increasing cognitive load just as deeper networks handle higher-dimensional data.
prototypes.notes: Lucas Pope's 2013 indie masterpiece turned bureaucratic drudgery into moral drama.

prototypes.name: one-minute-quest
prototypes.mechanic: 60-second adventure — Zelda-like exploration where you die every 60 seconds; items and progress persist across lives; solve the world in 60-second chunks
prototypes.ancestry: Minit (2018, Jan Willem Nijman / Kitty Calis / Jukio Kallio / Dominik Johann, Netherlands), The Legend of Zelda (1986, Nintendo)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move | Space — sword / interact | R — restart (lose all progress) | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 7 (Democratization — indie constraint-as-mechanic design)
prototypes.aiInsight: Minit weaponizes the roguelike death loop as a puzzle mechanic. The 60-second timer transforms a Zelda-like world into a constraint satisfaction problem — each life is a hypothesis about which path to take, which item to grab, which shortcut to unlock. Persistent progress across deaths turns death from failure into iteration. The Dutch team (Nijman from Vlambeer) brought arcade intensity to contemplative exploration.
prototypes.notes: The 60-second life reframes adventure as time management.

prototypes.name: fuzzy-quest
prototypes.mechanic: slapstick visual gag puzzle — a fuzzy creature chases a cherry through absurd scenarios; each screen is a visual gag requiring clicks in the right sequence
prototypes.ancestry: Chuchel (2018, Amanita Design, Jakub Dvorský, Prague, Czech Republic), Machinarium (2009, Amanita Design), Samorost (2003, Amanita Design)
prototypes.controls: Mouse — click objects to interact | R — restart scene | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 6 (Generative Play — authored absurdist comedy through interactive visual gags)
prototypes.aiInsight: Chuchel represents a shift from puzzle-solving to comedic interaction — every click produces a reaction, and the game rewards curiosity with humor. The design language is closer to interactive animation than traditional adventure games, presaging AI-driven procedural comedy.
prototypes.notes: Czech slapstick comedy point-and-click. Each scene is a self-contained visual gag.

prototypes.name: dream-walk
prototypes.mechanic: surreal dream exploration — walk through bizarre dream worlds with no objectives, no combat, no dialogue; collect effects that change your appearance; explore
prototypes.ancestry: Yume Nikki (2004, Kikiyama), LSD: Dream Emulator (1998, Asmik Ace)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move | Space — interact/collect | 9 — wake up | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 5 (Emergent Complexity — procedural worlds, player-driven meaning-making)
prototypes.aiInsight: Yume Nikki is an anti-game — it strips away objectives, scores, enemies, and dialogue to leave pure exploration. The player constructs meaning from surreal juxtapositions. This mirrors unsupervised learning: the system (player) finds patterns and clusters in unlabeled data (dream imagery) without explicit reward signals.
prototypes.notes: Surreal dream exploration. No objectives, no combat, no score.

prototypes.name: folk-thief
prototypes.mechanic: Korean folk hero action — play as Iljimae (Korean Robin Hood) in a side-scrolling action-platformer; steal from corrupt nobles, help the poor; Korea's earliest original game character
prototypes.ancestry: Adventures of Iljimae (1987, Aproman, Korea, MSX)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move/climb | Space — jump | Z — sword attack | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 2 (The Arcade Mind — action-platformer with character-driven narrative)
prototypes.aiInsight: Iljimae represents Korea's earliest original game character, predating most Asian game mascots. The Robin Hood archetype — steal from the rich, give to the poor — creates a moral feedback loop where the player's reward comes not from accumulation but from redistribution. This is an early example of prosocial game mechanics.
prototypes.notes: Side-scrolling action-platformer set in Joseon-era Korea. Iljimae wears a mask

prototypes.name: ur
prototypes.mechanic: race + dice + blocking — move pieces along shared track using dice rolls
prototypes.ancestry: Royal Game of Ur (~2600 BCE, Mesopotamia), Backgammon (~3000 BCE)
prototypes.controls: Click/1-7 — select piece to move | Space — roll dice | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 1 (Symbolic AI — tree search, graph algorithms)
prototypes.aiInsight: The Royal Game of Ur is a stochastic game with perfect information — the same class as backgammon. Expectiminimax (minimax with chance nodes) is the optimal algorithm. The shared middle track creates blocking and capture — emergent conflict from spatial constraints. TD-Gammon (1992) solved backgammon's cousin using temporal-difference learning, proving that ancient game designers had unknowingly created ideal RL training environments.
prototypes.notes: Oldest known board game with surviving rules (decoded by Irving Finkel from a cuneiform tablet).

prototypes.name: mancala
prototypes.mechanic: seed sowing — pick up seeds from a pit, distribute one-by-one around the circuit
prototypes.ancestry: Mancala/Oware (~700 CE, Africa/Asia), Kalah (1940, William Champion)
prototypes.controls: Click or 1-6 — choose pit to sow | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 1 (Symbolic AI — tree search, graph algorithms)
prototypes.aiInsight: Mancala has a single verb (sow) with positional consequences that cascade. Despite simple rules, the game tree is enormous — a solved game only for small configurations. The sowing mechanic is a discrete dynamical system where each action redistributes state across the board. Multi-step sowing (ending in your store = extra turn) creates lookahead chains that reward computational planning.
prototypes.notes: Single verb (sow) with positional consequences — elegant depth from one action.

prototypes.name: rank-battle
prototypes.mechanic: move hidden-rank pieces, bluff and deduce to capture flag
prototypes.ancestry: Stratego (1947, Jacques Johan Mogendorff/Milton Bradley), L'Attaque (1909)
prototypes.controls: Click — select piece and move | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 2 (Expert Systems — hidden information, deductive reasoning)
prototypes.aiInsight: Stratego is an imperfect information game where each combat reveals information — players build a probabilistic model of opponent piece identities through observation. This is Bayesian inference applied to adversarial game theory, where each engagement updates the belief state.
prototypes.notes: Hidden information creates a deduction game layered on tactical movement.

prototypes.name: chess
prototypes.mechanic: chess with animated piece captures against AI opponent
prototypes.ancestry: Battle Chess (1988, Interplay), Chess (~600 CE, Chaturanga, India)
prototypes.controls: Mouse — select piece/destination | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 1 (Symbolic AI — the foundational game of artificial intelligence)
prototypes.aiInsight: Chess is THE canonical problem in AI history. Shannon's 1950 paper on chess programming, Turing's paper machine, and Deep Blue's 1997 victory are milestones that defined each AI era. Every game tree search algorithm was first tested on chess.
prototypes.notes: Battle Chess added personality to captures — the game is the same, the spectacle is new.

prototypes.name: mahjong
prototypes.mechanic: tile matching — remove matching pairs of free tiles from a layered pyramid layout until all tiles are cleared
prototypes.ancestry: Mahjong (traditional Chinese), Mahjong Solitaire (1981 Activision), Shanghai (1986)
prototypes.controls: Click — select tile | H — hint | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 2 (Expert Systems — pattern matching, search)
prototypes.aiInsight: A tile is "free" only if it has no tiles on top and at least one open side. This creates a visibility-action gap — you can see tiles you can't act on. Planning requires reasoning about which removals unlock future moves. This is a constrained search problem where greedy matching often leads to dead ends.
prototypes.notes: Brodie Lockard created the computer version in 1981. Shanghai (1986) by Activision made it a mainstream hit. The layered tile layout creates a 2.5D spatial puzzle from 2D matching rules.

prototypes.name: reversi
prototypes.mechanic: flanking capture — place discs to outflank opponent's pieces in any direction; flanked pieces flip to your color
prototypes.ancestry: Reversi (1883 Lewis Waterman), Othello (1971 Goro Hasegawa), Logistello (1997)
prototypes.controls: Click — place disc | R — new game | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 4 (Expert Boom — Logistello defeated world champion 1997; positional evaluation)
prototypes.aiInsight: Corner squares are maximally valuable (can never be flipped), edges are strong, squares adjacent to corners are dangerous. This positional hierarchy maps directly to feature-weighted evaluation functions used in all game AI.
prototypes.notes: "A minute to learn, a lifetime to master." Four starting pieces in center. Must outflank at least one opponent piece to play. If no legal move, turn passes. Most pieces when board is full wins.

prototypes.name: dominoes
prototypes.mechanic: number chain matching — play tiles by matching their pip values to the open ends of the chain; first to empty your hand wins
prototypes.ancestry: Chinese Dominoes (~1200 CE), European Dominoes (~1700s Italy), Block Game, Draw Game
prototypes.controls: Click tile in hand — select | Click end of chain — play | D — draw from boneyard | R — new game | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 2 (Expert Systems — rule-based play, probabilistic tracking of unseen tiles)
prototypes.aiInsight: Dominoes with hidden tiles is a game of imperfect information. Expert play requires tracking which tiles remain unseen and inferring opponent's hand from their draws and passes. This is the same hidden-state inference that Bayesian filters perform.
prototypes.notes: Standard double-six set (28 tiles, values 0-6). Draw game variant: if you can't play, draw from the boneyard until you can. When no one can play and boneyard is empty, lowest pip total wins.

prototypes.name: fanorona
prototypes.mechanic: dual-direction capture — move a piece toward enemies to capture by approach OR move away to capture by withdrawal; chain captures possible
prototypes.ancestry: Fanorona (~1680, Madagascar), Alquerque (~1000 BCE, Middle East)
prototypes.controls: Mouse — select piece/destination | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 2 (traditional Malagasy ~1680)
prototypes.aiInsight: Fanorona's dual-direction capture mechanic creates a unique decision tree where every move has two possible interpretations. The approach/withdrawal choice doubles the branching factor compared to simple capture games, making it computationally interesting despite the small board.
prototypes.notes: Madagascar's national board game. The 9x5 board with mixed orthogonal and diagonal connections

prototypes.name: surakarta
prototypes.mechanic: orbital capture — 6×6 grid with curved corner loops; normal moves are one step; captures ONLY happen by traveling along curved loops to reach enemy pieces
prototypes.ancestry: Surakarta (traditional, Java, Indonesia)
prototypes.controls: Mouse — click piece then destination | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 2 (traditional Javanese court game with unique loop-based capture)
prototypes.aiInsight: Surakarta's orbital capture mechanic is unique in board game history — pieces can only be captured by traveling along curved loop paths at the board edges. This creates an entirely different spatial reasoning challenge from any other abstract strategy game.
prototypes.notes: The curved loops connecting board edges are the visual and mechanical signature.

prototypes.name: yutnori
prototypes.mechanic: stick-throwing race — throw 4 half-round sticks; flat/round combinations determine move distance (1-5 + bonus); race pieces around a square board with shortcut diagonal paths
prototypes.ancestry: Yut Nori (~2000 years old, Korea), Pachisi (India), Parcheesi
prototypes.controls: Space — throw sticks | Click — select piece to move | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 1 (Symbolic AI — tree search with stochastic elements)
prototypes.aiInsight: Yut Nori combines stochastic outcomes (stick throws) with strategic choice (which piece to move, whether to stack). The bonus throw mechanic for Yut(4) and Mo(5) creates chains of decisions reminiscent of multi-step planning in AI. Stacking pieces is an early example of risk-reward aggregation — concentrating value for speed at the cost of catastrophic capture.
prototypes.notes: Traditional Korean New Year game still widely played today.

prototypes.name: hnefatafl
prototypes.mechanic: asymmetric siege — King + 8 defenders start in center; 16 attackers surround; King wins by reaching a corner; attackers win by capturing the King; pieces move like rooks; capture by sandwiching
prototypes.ancestry: Hnefatafl (~400 CE, Norse/Sami), Tablut (Linnaeus 1732 documentation)
prototypes.controls: Mouse — select piece/destination | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 1 (Symbolic AI — asymmetric game trees, minimax with role-aware evaluation)
prototypes.aiInsight: Hnefatafl's asymmetry makes it a fascinating AI challenge — the two sides have fundamentally different objectives, requiring distinct evaluation functions. Unlike chess where both sides optimize the same goal, here the attacker maximizes encirclement while the defender maximizes escape routes.
prototypes.notes: The oldest known strategy board game of Scandinavia. Played across the Viking world from

prototypes.name: makruk
prototypes.mechanic: Thai chess variant — pawns on 3rd rank at start, promote to weak queens (one diagonal step); no long-range bishops; slower, more positional than Western chess
prototypes.ancestry: Chaturanga (~600 CE, India) → Makruk (~1000 CE, Thailand)
prototypes.controls: Mouse — select piece/destination | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 2 (traditional Thai ~1000 CE)
prototypes.aiInsight: Makruk is the closest living relative of the original Indian Chaturanga. Unlike Western chess, it preserved the weak queen and bishop, making it a slower, more positional game where endgame technique is paramount. The lack of long-range diagonal pieces forces close-quarters tactical play.
prototypes.notes: Makruk is still widely played in Thailand and is recognized by FIDE. The pieces have distinct Thai names and styling.

prototypes.name: janggi
prototypes.mechanic: Korean chess with cannons — 9×10 board with river and palaces; cannons MUST jump over exactly one piece to move or capture; generals confined to 3×3 palace
prototypes.ancestry: Janggi (~900 CE, Korea), Xiangqi (China), Chaturanga (~600 CE, India)
prototypes.controls: Mouse — select piece/destination | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 2 (traditional Korean board game, ~900 CE)
prototypes.aiInsight: Janggi's cannon mechanic — requiring a jump over exactly one piece to move or capture — creates a unique tactical dimension absent from Western chess. The cannon's power fluctuates wildly as pieces are captured, making material evaluation non-trivial for both humans and AI.
prototypes.notes: The cannon jumping mechanic is the unique selling point, creating totally different tactics from any other chess variant.

prototypes.name: togyzkumalak
prototypes.mechanic: mancala with tuzdyk — 2×9 pits with 162 stones; the tuzdyk rule permanently claims an opponent's pit when capturing exactly 3; alters the board topology permanently
prototypes.ancestry: Mancala/Oware (~700 CE, Africa/Asia), Togyzkumalak (Central Asia, traditional nomadic peoples)
prototypes.controls: Click pit to sow | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 2 (Expert Systems — heuristic rules, knowledge encoding)
prototypes.aiInsight: Togyzkumalak introduces a permanent board mutation — the tuzdyk — that has no parallel in Western mancala variants. Claiming an opponent's pit as your own permanently redirects future stone flow, creating an irreversible topological change. This mechanic encodes a deep heuristic: sometimes the most valuable move is one that alters the rules themselves. Traditional Central Asian nomadic peoples (UNESCO Intangible Heritage 2020).
prototypes.notes: The tuzdyk mechanic permanently alters board topology — unique among all mancala variants.

prototypes.name: congkak
prototypes.mechanic: mancala with store-in-circuit — the player's store IS included in the sowing circle; creates capture-your-own-store dynamics; skip opponent's store during sowing
prototypes.ancestry: Congkak/Sungka (~16th century, Malaysia/Philippines), Mancala family
prototypes.controls: Click — choose pit to sow | 1-7 — keyboard pit select | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 2 (Knowledge Engineering — rule-based systems, expert heuristics)
prototypes.aiInsight: Congkak's store-in-circuit sowing creates different tempo dynamics than African mancala. The extra pit and higher seed count expand the game tree considerably. Expert heuristics capture the importance of store-landing bonus turns and opposite-pit captures more naturally than brute-force search.
prototypes.notes: 2x7 board with 7 seeds per pit. Store-in-circuit sowing is the key differentiator.

prototypes.name: paddle-volley
prototypes.mechanic: deflect — ball bounces between two paddles, angles change based on hit position
prototypes.ancestry: Tennis for Two (1958), Magnavox Odyssey (1972), Atari Pong (1972)
prototypes.controls: W/S — left paddle up/down | Up/Down arrows — right paddle up/down | Space — serve | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 2 (Expert Systems — rule-based, no learning)
prototypes.aiInsight: Opponent is a Y-tracker if-statement — indistinguishable from contemporary AI capability. The ball's physics creates more emergent intelligence than the opponent's code. Specialization beats generality at every era.
prototypes.notes: The original deflection loop. Every angle game since Breakout lives inside this prototype.

prototypes.name: brick-break
prototypes.mechanic: deflect + destroy — ball clears destructible brick field; angles steepen as bricks clear
prototypes.ancestry: Pong (1972), Atari Breakout (1976), Arkanoid (1986)
prototypes.controls: Left/Right arrows or A/D — paddle | Space — launch | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 2 (Expert Systems — rule-based, no learning)
prototypes.aiInsight: No AI opponent at all — physics is the antagonist. DeepMind's DQN (2013) found the tunnel-behind-the-wall strategy humans knew intuitively but never formally documented until AI rediscovered it.
prototypes.notes: Introduces the destructible environment. The moment the brick field thins is when tension peaks — that's the core insight.

prototypes.name: shufflepuck
prototypes.mechanic: air hockey with perspective view and distinct opponent personalities
prototypes.ancestry: Shufflepuck Cafe (1989, Broderbund), Air Hockey (1972, Brunswick Billiards)
prototypes.controls: Mouse — move paddle | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — personality-driven AI opponents)
prototypes.aiInsight: Shufflepuck Cafe gave each opponent a distinct behavioral personality — varying reaction speed, aggressiveness, and strategy. This was an early form of AI personality modeling, where the same basic algorithm is parameterized differently to create perceivably different characters.
prototypes.notes: Perspective air hockey. Mouse controls player paddle at bottom.

prototypes.name: whackamole
prototypes.mechanic: reaction time targeting — moles pop up from holes randomly; click them before they disappear; speed increases over time
prototypes.ancestry: Mogura Taiji (1975 TOGO), Whac-A-Mole (1975 Aaron Fechter), arcade redemption games
prototypes.controls: Click mole — whack it | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 2-5 (Expert Systems → Statistical — reaction time models, attention allocation, Fitts's Law)
prototypes.aiInsight: Whac-A-Mole directly measures reaction time + motor precision — the two components of Fitts's Law (1954). As moles appear faster, the game transitions from a reaction task to an attention allocation task: you can't watch all holes simultaneously. This is the cocktail party problem — selective attention under information overload.
prototypes.notes: Aaron Fechter created the original Whac-A-Mole at Creative Engineering Inc. (1975). The game became the definitive arcade redemption game. The phrase "whack-a-mole" entered common language to describe futile reactive problem-solving.

prototypes.name: gem-aim
prototypes.mechanic: competitive Breakout — bounce a ball to break gems on your side; cleared gems become attacks raining on opponent's field; first to lose their ball loses
prototypes.ancestry: Breakout (1976, Atari) → Arkanoid (1986, Taito) → Puchi Carat (1997, Taito)
prototypes.controls: Mouse — move paddle | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 4 (Intelligence Explosion — competitive puzzle-action hybrid AI)
prototypes.aiInsight: Puchi Carat merged Breakout with competitive puzzle-game attack mechanics. The AI opponent must balance offense (clearing gem clusters for attacks) with defense (surviving incoming garbage). This dual-objective optimization presages modern game AI that must weigh multiple competing goals simultaneously.
prototypes.notes: Split-screen competitive Breakout. Player (left) vs AI (right).

prototypes.name: tontie
prototypes.mechanic: numpad whacking — enemies appear on a 3×3 grid mapped to the numpad; press the corresponding key to hammer them; speed increases; miss = damage
prototypes.ancestry: Tontie (2004, On Nakayama / Eyezmaze), Whac-A-Mole (1975, Aaron Fechter), Flash browser games (2000s Japan)
prototypes.controls: 1-9 (numpad or top row) — hammer that cell | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 4 (Statistical Learning — reaction mapping, spatial memory, pattern recognition under time pressure)
prototypes.aiInsight: Tontie maps a 3×3 spatial grid to the numpad, creating a learned motor mapping between visual space and finger positions. This is a form of stimulus-response compatibility — the numpad's physical layout mirrors the game grid, making the mapping intuitive once learned. The game becomes a test of how fast your brain can translate visual position to motor action.
prototypes.notes: On Nakayama (Eyezmaze) created Tontie as a Flash game in 2004, alongside the famous GROW series. The numpad control scheme was innovative — instead of clicking moles, you hammered enemies by feel. This made it a typing-game hybrid where spatial awareness mapped to muscle memory.

prototypes.name: flap-duel
prototypes.mechanic: flap-to-fly — height advantage wins lance combat, gravity always pulls, flap to rise
prototypes.ancestry: Joust (1982, Williams Electronics), Balloon Fight (1984, Nintendo)
prototypes.controls: Space — flap (gain height) | Left/Right arrows — move | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Early Neural — enemies patrol with simple state machines, egg mechanic adds depth)
prototypes.aiInsight: Height-wins is elegant because it reduces combat to a single axis of advantage. The flap mechanic creates emergent difficulty curves — mastering momentum IS mastering the game. No health bars needed when physics determines victory.
prototypes.notes: The first fighting game built on physics rather than hitboxes. Flap-to-fly created a vocabulary that Balloon Fight, Flappy Bird, and countless others would inherit.

prototypes.name: kung-fu
prototypes.mechanic: side-scrolling beat-em-up — punch and kick through enemy waves
prototypes.ancestry: Kung-Fu Master (1984, Takashi Nishiyama, Irem), Karate Champ (1984, Data East)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move | Z — punch | X — kick | Space — jump | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — wave-based enemy spawning patterns)
prototypes.aiInsight: Kung-Fu Master's wave spawning system uses simple frequency and direction rules to create escalating difficulty — an early form of procedural difficulty adjustment that anticipated modern dynamic difficulty systems.
prototypes.notes: Walk-and-fight loop is the ancestor of all beat-em-ups.

prototypes.name: monster-smash
prototypes.mechanic: kaiju destruction — punch buildings, eat people, dodge military
prototypes.ancestry: Rampage (1986, Brian Colin, Bally Midway), King of the Monsters (1991, SNK)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move/climb | Z — punch | X — eat | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — destructible environment systems)
prototypes.aiInsight: Rampage's building destruction uses a tile-based damage model where each section can be independently destroyed, creating emergent collapse patterns. This granular destruction prefigured voxel-based damage systems and physics-driven demolition in modern games.
prototypes.notes: Power fantasy of being the monster inverts typical arcade player-victim dynamic.

prototypes.name: martial-run
prototypes.mechanic: cinematic stance-based martial arts with running and fighting modes
prototypes.ancestry: Karateka (1984, Jordan Mechner, Broderbund), The Way of the Exploding Fist (1985, Beam Software)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move/aim | Space — toggle stance | Z — punch | X — kick | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — rotoscoped animation in games)
prototypes.aiInsight: Karateka was the first game to use rotoscoped animation for fluid character movement, filmed from Jordan Mechner's karate-trained father. This human-motion-to-game-animation pipeline prefigured motion capture technology and the pose estimation systems that modern ML uses for animation.
prototypes.notes: The stance toggle creates a deliberate rhythm between movement and combat.

prototypes.name: arena-fighter
prototypes.mechanic: 1v1 fighting with special moves, combo system, and finishing moves
prototypes.ancestry: Mortal Kombat (1992, Ed Boon/John Tobias, Midway), Street Fighter II (1991, Capcom)
prototypes.controls: A/D — move | W — jump | S — crouch | J — punch | K — kick | L — block | Special: ↓→J — projectile, ↓→K — special | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — input-reading AI with difficulty scaling)
prototypes.aiInsight: Fighting game AI must balance between being beatable and being challenging — early fighting game AI like MK's read player inputs with a delay window, creating the illusion of reaction. At higher difficulties, the AI reads inputs faster, effectively cheating — a controversial but effective difficulty scaling technique.
prototypes.notes: 1v1 fighting game. Health bars, special moves, round-based.

prototypes.name: teeworlds
prototypes.mechanic: 2D platform combat with grappling hook physics and weapon pickups
prototypes.ancestry: Teeworlds (2007, Magnus Auvinen), Soldat (2002, Michal Marcinkowski), Liero (1998, Joosa Riekkinen)
prototypes.controls: A/D — move | W — jump | Mouse — aim | Click — shoot | Right-click — grapple | 1/2/3 — weapons | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 5 (Deep Learning — physics-based movement mastery, momentum-aware AI)
prototypes.aiInsight: Teeworlds' grappling hook transformed 2D combat from platform-hopping to physics-based acrobatics. The hook creates a continuous action space for movement — exactly the kind of high-dimensional control problem that deep reinforcement learning excels at but rule-based AI struggles with.
prototypes.notes: Grapple-based movement is the core innovation. The skill ceiling comes from

prototypes.name: invaders
prototypes.mechanic: fix + suppress — hold a fixed position, eliminate descending formation before it reaches you
prototypes.ancestry: Taito Space Invaders (1978), Galaxian (1979), Galaga (1981)
prototypes.controls: Left/Right arrows or A/D — move | Space — shoot | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — rule-based, pre-backpropagation)
prototypes.aiInsight: Speedup-as-aliens-die is emergent curriculum learning — automatic difficulty escalation as the player succeeds. AI researchers wouldn't formalize curriculum learning until ~2015. Taito's accidental bug was 37 years ahead of the field.
prototypes.notes: The descending formation that speeds up as it shrinks — that escalating tempo is the actual design genius.

prototypes.name: bug-shoot
prototypes.mechanic: trackball shooter — destroy segmented centipede while mushroom field reshapes
prototypes.ancestry: Centipede (1981, Dona Bailey/Ed Logg, Atari), Millipede (1982, Atari)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys or WASD — move | Space — shoot | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — rule-based, pre-backpropagation)
prototypes.aiInsight: Player actions reshape the battlefield — every shot that hits a centipede segment spawns a mushroom, making future paths less predictable. This is implicit environment co-design between player and game system, decades before the concept of environment shaping in RL.
prototypes.notes: Dona Bailey was one of the first women to design an arcade game.

prototypes.name: sky-defense
prototypes.mechanic: area defense — target cursor-guided explosions to intercept incoming missiles
prototypes.ancestry: Missile Command (1980, Dave Theurer, Atari), Missile Defense 3-D (1983, Sega)
prototypes.controls: Mouse — aim cursor | Left click — fire from left base | Right click — fire from right base | Middle click/Space — fire from center | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — rule-based, pre-backpropagation)
prototypes.aiInsight: Missile Command is a resource allocation problem under escalating load — defend N cities with M missiles against increasing ICBM waves. The player must decide which threats to intercept and which to sacrifice. This is the defender's dilemma formalized: limited defense resources against unlimited offense. Game theory's asymmetric warfare before the term existed in gaming.
prototypes.notes: Defend cities under escalating ICBM barrage — resource management meets real-time targeting.

prototypes.name: pop-defense
prototypes.mechanic: place fixed towers to pop balloons following a winding path
prototypes.ancestry: Bloons TD (2007, Ninja Kiwi), Desktop Tower Defense (2007, HandDrawnGames), Rampart (1990, Atari)
prototypes.controls: Mouse — select and place towers, click to upgrade | Space — start next wave | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 5 (Deep Learning — optimal tower placement as spatial optimization)
prototypes.aiInsight: Tower defense games are constraint satisfaction puzzles in real-time clothing. The optimal placement of fixed-position agents to intercept moving targets along known paths is equivalent to the facility location problem in operations research — NP-hard in general but approximable through greedy heuristics.
prototypes.notes: Tower defense in its purest form. Path is the constraint,

prototypes.name: tower-defense
prototypes.mechanic: fixed-spot tower defense with four tower types, upgrades, and controllable hero
prototypes.ancestry: Kingdom Rush (2011, Ironhide), WarCraft III custom maps (2003), Plants vs. Zombies (2009, PopCap)
prototypes.controls: Mouse — place/upgrade towers, move hero | Space — next wave | 1-4 — select tower type | H — hero ability | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 5 (Deep Learning — unit composition as multi-objective optimization)
prototypes.aiInsight: Kingdom Rush added the hero unit to tower defense, giving the player one mobile piece on an otherwise static board. This single controllable agent amid automated defenses mirrors the human-in-the-loop paradigm in AI systems — most decisions are automated, but critical interventions require human judgment.
prototypes.notes: Tower defense with the addition of a controllable hero creates a dual-attention

prototypes.name: gorillas
prototypes.mechanic: artillery trajectory — two gorillas on skyscrapers throw exploding bananas; set angle and velocity; buildings are destructible; wind affects trajectory
prototypes.ancestry: Gorillas (1991, IBM / bundled with MS-DOS 5.0 as QBasic example), Scorched Earth (1991, Wendell Hicken), Artillery (1976, Mike Forman)
prototypes.controls: Left/Right — adjust angle | Up/Down — adjust velocity | Space — fire banana | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 2 (Expert Systems — ballistic calculation with adjustable parameters)
prototypes.aiInsight: Artillery games reduce to parametric projectile physics — angle θ and velocity v₀ define a parabolic arc modified by gravity and wind. The AI opponent solves this inverse kinematics problem with deliberate error injection to simulate human imperfection.
prototypes.notes: The original Gorillas shipped as GORILLA.BAS with QBasic on MS-DOS 5.0,

prototypes.name: fortress
prototypes.mechanic: team artillery — set angle and power, fire projectiles with wind; destructible terrain; special weapons; Korea's most beloved casual PC game of the PC bang era
prototypes.ancestry: Fortress (1999, CCR Inc., Korea), Scorched Earth (1991, Wendell Hicken), Worms (1995, Team17), Gorillas (1991, IBM)
prototypes.controls: Up/Down — adjust angle | Space (hold/release) — set power | 1-4 — select weapon | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 4 (Machine Learning — wind-compensated trajectory planning with weapon selection heuristics)
prototypes.aiInsight: Fortress elevated artillery games with wind as a core variable — every shot requires recalculating the parabolic arc under changing conditions. The AI must solve a noisy optimization problem each turn, balancing weapon choice, wind compensation, and terrain destruction strategy.
prototypes.notes: Fortress (포트리스) was THE game of Korean PC bangs from 1999-2009. Its team-based

prototypes.name: archon
prototypes.mechanic: chess-like board strategy with real-time arena combat for captures
prototypes.ancestry: Archon: The Light and the Dark (1983, Free Fall Associates/EA), Battle Chess (1988, Interplay)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move/aim | Space — select/attack | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — hybrid strategic and tactical AI)
prototypes.aiInsight: Archon requires two fundamentally different AI competencies in one game — strategic board evaluation (like chess engines) and real-time combat reflexes (like action game AI). This dual-reasoning architecture prefigures modern hierarchical AI systems that combine strategic planners with reactive controllers.
prototypes.notes: The genre hybrid creates a game where chess skill and action skill both matter.

prototypes.name: autobattler
prototypes.mechanic: auto-battler — buy and position units on a grid during a planning phase; units then fight automatically, with synergies between unit types driving strategy
prototypes.ancestry: Auto Chess (2019 Dota mod), Teamfight Tactics (2019), Super Auto Pets (2021)
prototypes.controls: Click shop — buy unit | Drag unit — position on board | Click sell — sell unit | Space — start battle | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 7 (Generative-Agentic — multi-agent systems, emergent strategy)
prototypes.aiInsight: Auto-battlers separate strategy from execution. The player designs a system (unit composition and positioning) then watches it execute autonomously. This is the core challenge of AI alignment: specifying objectives and constraints rather than actions. The emergent combat behavior from simple unit rules mirrors how complex behavior emerges from simple agent specifications.
prototypes.notes: Auto Chess (2019) Dota 2 mod by Drodo Studio created the genre overnight. The key insight: removing direct control forces strategic thinking at a higher abstraction level. Players must reason about unit synergies, economy management, and positioning — pure system design.

prototypes.name: paradroid
prototypes.mechanic: body possession — you're the weakest droid; take over stronger droids by winning a circuit-board hacking minigame
prototypes.ancestry: Paradroid (1985, Andrew Braybrook/Hewson), Paradroid 90 (1990, Graftgold)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move | Space — bump into droid to hack / activate node in hack mode | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — AI opponents with circuit-board decision making)
prototypes.aiInsight: Paradroid's hacking minigame is a real-time resource allocation problem — both sides compete to claim nodes in a circuit graph. The AI must balance offensive activation with defensive blocking, a microcosm of adversarial planning that prefigures modern competitive AI agents.
prototypes.notes: The genre hybrid merges top-down exploration with puzzle-combat for possession.

prototypes.name: getoverit
prototypes.mechanic: rage climbing — climb a mountain using only a sledgehammer from a cauldron; mouse controls the hammer; no checkpoints; any mistake can send you back to the start
prototypes.ancestry: Getting Over It with Bennett Foddy (2017, Bennett Foddy)
prototypes.controls: Mouse — aim hammer | Hold click — grip/push surfaces | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 6 (Generative Explosion — physics-based rage games as emotional design)
prototypes.aiInsight: Getting Over It demonstrates that game AI isn't just about opponents — the terrain itself is an adversarial intelligence. Every surface is designed to punish overconfidence and reward patience. The physics simulation creates emergent difficulty that no scripted AI could match, making the mountain a perfect adversary that never cheats but always wins.
prototypes.notes: The sledgehammer-from-a-cauldron mechanic is deceptively simple — mouse aim plus physics.

prototypes.name: okhlos
prototypes.mechanic: mob control — recruit citizens into an angry mob; the mob surges, scatters, and reforms as a physics-based swarm; storm buildings and fight mythological bosses
prototypes.ancestry: Okhlos (2016, Coffee Powered Machine/Devolver Digital), Pikmin (2001, Nintendo)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move philosopher | Space/Click — direct mob attack | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 6 (Deep Learning — emergent swarm behavior from simple boid rules, physics-based crowd simulation)
prototypes.aiInsight: Okhlos demonstrates that complex crowd behavior emerges from simple rules applied to many agents — a core principle of swarm intelligence. Each citizen follows basic flocking rules (separation, alignment, cohesion toward the leader) yet the mob as a whole exhibits organic surging, scattering, and reforming behavior that feels alive.
prototypes.notes: Argentine indie game by Coffee Powered Machine (Buenos Aires, 2016). Published by Devolver Digital.

prototypes.name: nidhogg
prototypes.mechanic: fencing tug-of-war — 1v1 sword fighting where killing your opponent lets you advance toward their side; they respawn and fight back; first to reach the opposing end wins
prototypes.ancestry: Nidhogg (2014, Messhof), Divekick (2013, One True Game Studios), Bushido Blade (1997, Square)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move | W — jump | Space — attack | Up/Down — sword stance | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 6 (Generative Explosion — emergent competitive AI through simple stance mechanics)
prototypes.aiInsight: Nidhogg demonstrates how minimal combat systems (three sword heights) create deep emergent gameplay through rock-paper-scissors-like stance interactions. The tug-of-war respawn mechanic inverts the death penalty — dying is a setback, not a loss — creating sustained tension that modern competitive AI must learn to navigate.
prototypes.notes: Creator Mark Essen / Messhof (USA, 2014). Fencing tug-of-war where momentum shifts with every kill.

prototypes.name: bishibashi
prototypes.mechanic: rapid minigame compilation — wacky 10-30 second minigames in rapid succession; button mashing, timing, pattern matching in absurd Japanese scenarios
prototypes.ancestry: Bishi Bashi Special (1996, Konami), WarioWare (2003, Nintendo), Incredible Machine (1993, Sierra)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move/dodge | Space — action/mash | 1-4 — color buttons | 0-9 — number entry | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 4 (Deep Learning — pattern recognition across rapidly shifting game contexts)
prototypes.aiInsight: Bishi Bashi's rapid minigame format is a fascinating AI challenge — an agent must instantly recognize a new game context, identify the correct strategy, and execute with precision, all within seconds. This mirrors transfer learning and few-shot adaptation in modern AI. The compilation format also demonstrates how simple mechanics become complex through rapid context-switching.
prototypes.notes: Creator Konami (Japan, 1996+ arcade). Precursor to WarioWare's microgame format.

prototypes.name: chabudai
prototypes.mechanic: rage table-flipping — endure frustrating dinner scenarios building a rage meter; when full, FLIP THE TABLE sending everything flying with physics; distance and destruction = score
prototypes.ancestry: Cho Chabudai Gaeshi (2009, Taito, Japan-only arcade), Incredible Crisis (1999, Tokuma Shoten)
prototypes.controls: Space — flip table at peak rage | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 6 (Creative Explosion — pure catharsis as game mechanic)
prototypes.aiInsight: Cho Chabudai Gaeshi is remarkable for making the player's emotional arc the entire game loop. The frustration-building phase is deliberately tedious — the game weaponizes boredom against the player, making the cathartic release of flipping the table genuinely satisfying. This is one of the earliest examples of a game designing for emotional payoff rather than skill mastery.
prototypes.notes: The original arcade cabinet had a physical table edge the player would slam upward.

prototypes.name: skul
prototypes.mechanic: skull-swapping roguelite — swap skulls to change your entire moveset; each skull is a different class with unique attacks; combine two skull abilities for tactical advantage
prototypes.ancestry: Skul: The Hero Slayer (2021, SouthPAW Games, Korea), Dead Cells (2018, Motion Twin), Mega Man (1987, Capcom)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move/jump | Space — attack | Z — special ability | Tab — swap skull | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 7 (Integration — Korean indie roguelite with identity-swapping combat system)
prototypes.aiInsight: Skul inverts the roguelite power fantasy by making the player character disposable — your body is a skeleton, power comes from wearing different skulls. This modular identity system creates emergent class-switching strategy reminiscent of job systems but in real-time combat. The Korean indie scene produced several globally successful roguelites by combining accessible action with deep systemic depth.
prototypes.notes: SouthPAW Games is a small Korean studio that achieved global success with Skul.

prototypes.name: road-crossing
prototypes.mechanic: grid traversal — cross moving lanes of traffic and ride logs across a river
prototypes.ancestry: Frogger (1981, Konami), Freeway (1981, Activision)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys or WASD — move | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — rule-based, pre-backpropagation)
prototypes.aiInsight: Frogger is a rhythm-matching problem disguised as a maze game — each lane is a timing window. Modern RL agents struggle with Frogger because the reward signal is sparse and the state space demands long-horizon planning across multiple independent timers.
prototypes.notes: Distinct from Pac-Man: no maze walls, no pursuers. The obstacle IS the path.

prototypes.name: ice-slide
prototypes.mechanic: push ice blocks to crush enemies in frozen maze
prototypes.ancestry: Pengo (1982, Sega)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move | Space — push/kick block | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — pattern recognition, expert systems mature)
prototypes.aiInsight: Pengo's sliding-block crush mechanic is a planning problem where each push has cascading spatial consequences — the player must reason about trajectories through a dynamic obstacle field, anticipating both block physics and enemy pathfinding simultaneously.
prototypes.notes: Ice block pushing creates emergent strategy from simple rules.

prototypes.name: tunnel-digger
prototypes.mechanic: tunnel through dirt, pump enemies until they pop
prototypes.ancestry: Dig Dug (1982, Shouichi Fukatani, Namco), Mr. Do! (1982, Universal)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move/dig | Space — pump/inflate | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — early spatial reasoning)
prototypes.aiInsight: Dig Dug's enemies exhibit two behavioral modes — normal pathfinding and ghost-mode wall penetration — an early example of state-machine AI with environment-dependent transitions, predating modern context-aware agent architectures.
prototypes.notes: Tunneling mechanic creates emergent strategy as player literally shapes the level.

prototypes.name: light-trail
prototypes.mechanic: light cycle trails — wall off opponent, avoid crashing
prototypes.ancestry: Tron (1982, Bally Midway), Snafu (1978, Mattel), Blockade (1976, Gremlin)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — change direction | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — adversarial spatial reasoning)
prototypes.aiInsight: Light cycle games create a pure adversarial territory-denial problem. The shrinking free space is a constraint satisfaction problem that becomes harder each frame — prefiguring the bounded-resource reasoning central to real-time AI planning.
prototypes.notes: Territory denial through trail permanence creates escalating tension.

prototypes.name: nightstalker
prototypes.mechanic: top-down maze shooter with limited ammo, stealth evasion, and escalating threats
prototypes.ancestry: Night Stalker (1982, Mattel/Intellivision), Berzerk (1980, Stern Electronics)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move | Space — shoot | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 2 (Expert Systems — state-machine enemy behavior)
prototypes.aiInsight: Night Stalker's enemies have distinct behavioral states (patrol, chase, retreat) creating emergent difficulty through interaction — an early example of multi-agent systems where simple individual rules produce complex collective behavior.
prototypes.notes: Top-down maze shooter. Limited ammo forces strategic play.

prototypes.name: locknchase
prototypes.mechanic: collect coins in maze while locking doors behind you to trap pursuers
prototypes.ancestry: Lock 'n' Chase (1981, Data East), Pac-Man (1980, Namco)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move | Space — lock door | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 2 (Expert Systems — pursuit algorithms with obstacle avoidance)
prototypes.aiInsight: Lock 'n' Chase adds player-created obstacles to the pursuit game — the ability to reshape the maze in real-time transforms a pure evasion problem into a planning problem, presaging dynamic environment manipulation in modern AI research.
prototypes.notes: Pac-Man variant with door-locking mechanic. Collect all coins to clear level.

prototypes.name: capturetheflag
prototypes.mechanic: team-based flag capture in divided arena with tagging and jail mechanics
prototypes.ancestry: Capture the Flag (traditional playground game), Stratego (1947, Jumbo), Team Fortress (1996, id Software mod)
prototypes.controls: WASD — move | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 5 (Deep Learning — multi-agent coordination, role assignment)
prototypes.aiInsight: CTF requires agents to adopt roles (attacker, defender, support) and switch dynamically based on game state. This multi-agent coordination problem — where individual optimal play differs from team-optimal play — is a foundational challenge in cooperative AI research.
prototypes.notes: CTF distills team strategy to its essence: territory, roles, risk.

prototypes.name: tile-puzzle
prototypes.mechanic: tile puzzle navigation — collect keys, push blocks, navigate ice and force floors, avoid enemies to reach the exit; 149 levels of elegant puzzle design
prototypes.ancestry: Chip's Challenge (1989, Epyx/Chuck Sommerville), Sokoban (1982, Thinking Rabbit), Adventures of Lolo (1989, HAL Laboratory)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move | R — restart level | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Expert Systems — deterministic tile interactions create emergent puzzle complexity from simple rule sets)
prototypes.aiInsight: Each tile type follows a single deterministic rule, yet their combinations produce puzzles requiring planning, sequencing, and spatial reasoning. This compositional complexity from simple rules mirrors expert system design — small rule bases generating rich behavior.
prototypes.notes: Chuck Sommerville created Chip's Challenge for the Atari Lynx in 1989.

prototypes.name: laberinto
prototypes.mechanic: knowledge maze — navigate mazes while answering trivia questions; correct answers open paths; wrong answers spawn obstacles
prototypes.ancestry: Laberinto del Saber (Cuban educational games, 1990s), Carmen Sandiego (1985, Broderbund), Maze Craze (1977, Atari)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move | 1-4 — answer questions | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Expert Systems — rule-based knowledge testing, adaptive difficulty through maze generation)
prototypes.aiInsight: The knowledge-as-key mechanic transforms trivia into spatial navigation. Each question becomes a lock — correct answers literally open paths forward. This maps knowledge representation onto physical space, a principle used in early expert systems where decision trees guided users through branching problem spaces.
prototypes.notes: Cuban educational game developers pioneered the fusion of maze navigation

prototypes.name: okada
prototypes.mechanic: traffic runner — dodge danfo buses, hawkers, potholes, and pedestrians while riding a motorcycle taxi (okada) through Lagos streets; collect passengers for points
prototypes.ancestry: Frogger (1981, Konami), Road Fighter (1984, Konami), Okada Ride (2018, Maliyo Games, Lagos, Nigeria)
prototypes.controls: Left/Right Arrow — change lane | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 6 (Generative — procedural traffic patterns, cultural context generation)
prototypes.aiInsight: Traffic runner games model flow dynamics and obstacle avoidance in constrained lanes. The Lagos traffic setting introduces culturally specific obstacle types and behavioral patterns that challenge AI systems trained on Western traffic assumptions.
prototypes.notes: The streets of Lagos as gameplay — vibrant colors, busy market atmosphere.

prototypes.name: spin-navigate
prototypes.mechanic: rotating stick navigation — guide a constantly rotating stick (helirin) through narrow maze passages; the stick rotates automatically, you control only position
prototypes.ancestry: Kuru Kuru Kururin (2001, Nintendo/Eighting, GBA — Japan/Europe only)
prototypes.controls: Arrow Keys — move helirin | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 5 (Reasoning — spatial-temporal planning around periodic rotation cycles)
prototypes.aiInsight: Rotating stick navigation creates a unique constraint satisfaction problem where the agent must plan trajectories that account for periodic geometric transformations. The continuously rotating elongated hitbox turns simple pathfinding into a time-dependent spatial reasoning challenge.
prototypes.notes: GBA launch title in Japan (2001), never released in North America.

prototypes.name: burrow-puzzle
prototypes.mechanic: two-layer puzzle — a mole navigates puzzles on surface and underground; dig holes to transition between layers; push a ball to the exit on each screen
prototypes.ancestry: Mole Mania (1996, Shigeru Miyamoto, Nintendo), Sokoban (1982, Thinking Rabbit)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move | Space — dig down / dig up | R — restart level | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — spatial reasoning across layers)
prototypes.aiInsight: Mole Mania's two-layer puzzle design anticipates multi-state search problems where the solution requires transitioning between parallel state spaces — a concept now fundamental to hierarchical planning in AI.
prototypes.notes: Miyamoto's hidden gem — a Game Boy puzzle masterpiece that sold poorly but influenced spatial puzzle design.

prototypes.name: bomb-battle
prototypes.mechanic: water balloon bombing — drop water balloons that burst in cross patterns; trap opponents in bubbles; collect power-ups to increase range and balloon count
prototypes.ancestry: Bomberman (1983, Hudson Soft), Dynablaster (1990, Hudson Soft), Crazy Arcade / BnB (2001, Nexon)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move | Space — drop balloon | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 5 (Deep Learning — strategic trap-setting, pathfinding around hazards)
prototypes.aiInsight: Balloon bombing requires planning multi-step traps — predicting opponent movement and cutting off escape routes. This combines real-time pathfinding with game-tree search, as each balloon placement reshapes the navigation graph. Korea's decade-long obsession with BnB created enormous datasets of human strategic play.
prototypes.notes: Nexon's Crazy Arcade (2001) became Korea's #1 casual game for a decade.

prototypes.name: lunar-lander
prototypes.mechanic: gravity landing — thrust against gravity with finite fuel to land softly
prototypes.ancestry: Lunar Lander (1979, Atari), Thrust (1986, Jeremy Smith)
prototypes.controls: Up arrow or W — thrust | Left/Right or A/D — rotate | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 2 (Expert Systems — rule-based, no learning)
prototypes.aiInsight: Lunar Lander is a continuous control problem with a hard constraint (fuel) and a soft constraint (landing speed). It's the archetype of the powered descent guidance problem that NASA solved analytically for Apollo — optimal control theory applied to games before anyone called it that.
prototypes.notes: Pure physics puzzle — no enemies, no maze. Just gravity, fuel, and the ground.

prototypes.name: marble-roll
prototypes.mechanic: roll marble through isometric course with physics and obstacles
prototypes.ancestry: Marble Madness (1984, Mark Cerny, Atari), Cameltry (1989, Taito)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — tilt/accelerate marble | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — real-time physics simulation)
prototypes.aiInsight: Marble Madness was the first arcade game to use a full physics simulation for its core mechanic. The trackball-to-momentum mapping presaged modern physics engines and the continuous-state control problems that deep reinforcement learning would later tackle.
prototypes.notes: Isometric perspective with real momentum creates satisfying skill curve.

prototypes.name: pinball
prototypes.mechanic: flipper-controlled ball physics with bumpers, ramps, and targets
prototypes.ancestry: Pinball Dreams (1992, Digital Illusions), Visual Pinball (1999, Randy Davis)
prototypes.controls: Z/Left — left flipper | X/Right — right flipper | Space — launch ball | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — real-time physics simulation)
prototypes.aiInsight: Pinball simulation requires accurate real-time physics — ball-flipper collision response, gravity, friction, and elastic bouncing. Digital Illusions' engine was one of the first to make convincing pinball physics accessible on home computers, demonstrating that realistic simulation could be entertaining.
prototypes.notes: Pinball table with flippers, bumpers, drop targets, and ramps.

prototypes.name: artillery-duel
prototypes.mechanic: turn-based artillery with destructible terrain and wind physics
prototypes.ancestry: Worms (1995, Team17), Scorched Earth (1991, Wendell Hicken), Gorillas (1991, Microsoft QBasic)
prototypes.controls: Left/Right — move worm | Up/Down — aim angle | Space (hold) — charge power | Enter — fire | 1/2/3 — select weapon | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 4 (Statistical Learning — trajectory prediction, destructible environment state)
prototypes.aiInsight: Worms' destructible terrain turned the environment from static backdrop into dynamic game state. Every shot permanently altered the possibility space — a concept that parallels how reinforcement learning agents must adapt to non-stationary environments.
prototypes.notes: Turn-based artillery with terrain destruction. Wind and power create

prototypes.name: tower
prototypes.mechanic: physics stacking — drop blocks to build the tallest tower possible; overhanging parts break off, making each level narrower
prototypes.ancestry: Jenga (1983 Leslie Scott), Tetris stacking, mobile stacking games, World's Biggest (2012)
prototypes.controls: Click or Space — drop block | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 6 (Deep Learning — physics simulation, structural stability, robot block stacking)
prototypes.aiInsight: Tower stacking is a structural engineering problem: center of mass must stay over the base. Each imperfect placement narrows the effective platform, raising the difficulty exponentially. MIT's robot block-stacking research uses the same physics reasoning — predict stability from visual observation, then act.
prototypes.notes: Leslie Scott created Jenga in 1983 from a family game played in Ghana. This prototype simplifies to dropping blocks from above rather than removing from below. The tension comes from watching your tower grow while the platform shrinks with each imperfect drop. Simple mechanic, infinite replayability.

prototypes.name: goobridge
prototypes.mechanic: living physics bridge building — connect goo balls into structures to span gaps and reach the exit pipe; goo has spring physics
prototypes.ancestry: World of Goo (2008, Kyle Gabler & Ron Carmel / 2D Boy), Bridge Builder (2000, Alex Austin / Chronic Logic), The Incredible Machine (1993, Kevin Ryan / Sierra)
prototypes.controls: Click — pick up goo ball | Drag — position | Release — attach to nearby goo | R — restart level | N — next level (after win) | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 5-6 (Ensemble & Deep Learning — structural stability prediction, physics simulation, soft-body dynamics)
prototypes.aiInsight: World of Goo is a structural engineering problem disguised as a toy. Each goo ball placement is a decision about load distribution, and the spring-joint network must maintain structural integrity under gravity. This parallels finite element analysis in engineering and graph neural networks that predict physical system behavior from connectivity patterns.
prototypes.notes: Kyle Gabler and Ron Carmel created World of Goo at 2D Boy in 2008, evolving from Gabler's student project Tower of Goo. The game proved that physics puzzles could be emotionally engaging — the goo balls feel alive, creating attachment to your building material. This prototype captures the core loop: pick up, stretch, attach, watch it sway.

prototypes.name: roll-collect
prototypes.mechanic: sticky ball rolling — roll a ball that picks up objects; everything you collect makes the ball bigger, letting you collect bigger things; exponential growth from thumbtacks to houses
prototypes.ancestry: Katamari Damacy (2004, Keita Takahashi / Namco, PS2), Marble Madness (1984, Mark Cerny / Atari), Pac-Man (1980, Namco — collect-everything archetype)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — roll katamari | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 6 (Deep Learning — spatial reasoning for pickup eligibility, growth modeling, emergent physics from simple sticky-contact rule)
prototypes.aiInsight: Katamari's genius is a single recursive rule — absorb anything smaller, grow, repeat. This mirrors how neural networks learn hierarchical features: simple local patterns compose into increasingly complex structures. The exponential growth curve parallels how representational capacity scales with depth.
prototypes.notes: Top-down katamari rolling. Objects scattered across a large scrolling world.

prototypes.name: runner
prototypes.mechanic: platformer run — jump across gaps, stomp enemies, collect coins; gravity is the primary antagonist
prototypes.ancestry: Donkey Kong (1981), Super Mario Bros (1985), Sonic (1991)
prototypes.controls: Left/Right or A/D — run | Space or Up or W — jump (hold for higher) | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — rule-based, pre-backpropagation)
prototypes.aiInsight: Variable-height jump is continuous action control — infinite action space per jump. Coin placement is reward shaping: teaching through environmental design, the exact principle behind curriculum learning and reward shaping in RL.
prototypes.notes: Mario's genius: variable-height jump. The longer you hold, the higher you go. Depth from a single button.

prototypes.name: dig-and-run
prototypes.mechanic: dig traps — collect gold and trap enemies without jumping
prototypes.ancestry: Lode Runner (1983, Doug Smith/Broderbund), Championship Lode Runner (1984)
prototypes.controls: Left/Right or A/D — move | Up/W — climb | Down/S — descend | Z — dig left | X — dig right | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — rule-based, pre-backpropagation)
prototypes.aiInsight: The absence of jump IS the design — the player must use terrain modification (digging) as both traversal and trap mechanic. This is a planning problem where the action space includes environment modification. Enemies that can be temporarily trapped but regenerate create a temporal constraint on exploration. The level design becomes a puzzle where the solution path must account for dig-fill cycles.
prototypes.notes: Absence of jump is the design — trap mechanic + puzzle.

prototypes.name: whip-climb
prototypes.mechanic: whip through gothic castle with subweapons and stiff jumps
prototypes.ancestry: Castlevania (1986, Konami), Ghosts 'n Goblins (1985, Capcom)
prototypes.controls: ← → — move | ↑ ↓ — stairs/crouch | Space — jump | Z — whip | X — subweapon | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — scripted enemy patterns, level design as curriculum)
prototypes.aiInsight: Castlevania's deliberate movement constraints (stiff jumps, commitment to attacks) force the player to plan actions before executing them — each input is a commitment, not a correction. This mirrors the explore-exploit tradeoff where gathering information (observing patterns) must precede action (attacking).
prototypes.notes: Stiff jump physics are the design — they force commitment over reaction.

prototypes.name: bounty-explorer
prototypes.mechanic: explore interconnected map, acquire abilities to unlock paths
prototypes.ancestry: Metroid (1986, Nintendo/Gunpei Yokoi/Yoshio Sakamoto)
prototypes.controls: ← → — move | ↑ — aim up | Space — jump | Z — shoot | ↓ — morph ball (when acquired) | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — non-linear game structure, player-driven progression)
prototypes.aiInsight: Metroid's ability-gated world is a dependency graph disguised as geography — the player solves a topological sort problem by discovering which abilities unlock which paths. This is constraint satisfaction where the constraints are spatial and the variables are capabilities.
prototypes.notes: The key insight is abilities as keys — geography as lock.

prototypes.name: barrel-climb
prototypes.mechanic: climb ladders, dodge barrels, reach the top to rescue
prototypes.ancestry: Donkey Kong (1981, Shigeru Miyamoto, Nintendo), Space Panic (1980, Universal)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move/climb | Space — jump | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — goal-directed platforming)
prototypes.aiInsight: Donkey Kong introduced narrative goal structure to arcade games — the player has a visible objective (rescue) beyond score maximization. This goal-directed behavior model parallels the shift from reactive to planning-based AI agents.
prototypes.notes: The single-screen level design creates readable obstacle patterns.

prototypes.name: bubble-trap
prototypes.mechanic: trap enemies in bubbles on single-screen platforms, pop to defeat
prototypes.ancestry: Bubble Bobble (1986, Fukio Mitsuji, Taito), Snow Bros (1990, Toaplan)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move | Space — blow bubble | Up arrow — jump | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — emergent item systems)
prototypes.aiInsight: Bubble Bobble's item system uses hidden counters and specific action sequences to trigger rare power-ups — an early procedural reward system where player behavior unlocks content, prefiguring achievement systems and behavioral analytics in modern game design.
prototypes.notes: Two-step defeat mechanic (trap then pop) adds tactical depth to platforming.

prototypes.name: palace-runner
prototypes.mechanic: cinematic platformer with traps, ledge-grabbing, and sword combat
prototypes.ancestry: Prince of Persia (1989, Jordan Mechner, Broderbund), Karateka (1984, Jordan Mechner)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move/climb | Space — jump | Z — careful step | X — sword | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — rotoscoped animation and physics-based movement)
prototypes.aiInsight: Prince of Persia's rotoscoped animation created the first physics-plausible movement system in platformers — momentum, stumbling, ledge-grabbing. This commitment to physical realism in character control prefigured the motion-capture and physics-based animation that modern AI animation systems now generate.
prototypes.notes: The timer creates global urgency that transforms careful platforming into speed-running.

prototypes.name: jungle-swing
prototypes.mechanic: side-scroll platformer with vine swinging and hazard jumping
prototypes.ancestry: Pitfall! (1982, David Crane, Activision), Pitfall II (1984, Activision)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move/jump | Space — swing on vine | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 2 (Expert Systems — hardcoded level generation)
prototypes.aiInsight: Pitfall's 256 screens were generated by a polynomial counter (LFSR), making it one of the earliest examples of procedural content generation — a technique that would later become central to AI-driven game design.
prototypes.notes: Side-scrolling platformer. Jump over pits, swing on vines, avoid hazards.

prototypes.name: sky-archer
prototypes.mechanic: vertical scrolling platformer with archery combat and mythological setting
prototypes.ancestry: Kid Icarus (1986, Nintendo R&D1/Toru Osawa), Metroid (1986, Nintendo R&D1)
prototypes.controls: Left/Right — move | Up/Space — jump | Z — shoot arrow | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — pattern-based enemy spawning)
prototypes.aiInsight: Kid Icarus combined vertical platforming with RPG elements (health upgrades, weapon strength, shops) — an early example of genre hybridization that would later become standard. The vertical scroll direction was unusual and created unique spatial reasoning challenges.
prototypes.notes: Vertical scrolling platformer. Pit climbs upward, shooting enemies with arrows.

prototypes.name: platform-hop
prototypes.mechanic: auto-jump vertical platformer with procedural platforms and enemies
prototypes.ancestry: Doodle Jump (2009, Lima Sky), Ice Climber (1985, Nintendo), Icy Tower (2001, FreeLunchDesign)
prototypes.controls: Left/Right arrows — move | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 5 (Deep Learning — procedural generation mirrors generative models)
prototypes.aiInsight: Doodle Jump's infinite procedural generation solved the content problem by making each playthrough unique — a constraint-based generative approach that predated modern procedural generation research in deep learning.
prototypes.notes: Vertical endless platformer. Simple controls, infinite replayability.

prototypes.name: roof-sprint
prototypes.mechanic: one-button auto-runner — character runs automatically and accelerates; you can only jump; procedural cityscape with gaps and obstacles
prototypes.ancestry: Canabalt (2009, Adam Saltsman / Semi Secret Software), Pitfall! (1982, Activision), Moon Patrol (1982, Irem)
prototypes.controls: Space/Click — jump | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 5 (Deep Learning — one-button design presages neural architecture search for minimal viable control schemes)
prototypes.aiInsight: Canabalt's radical reduction to a single input mirrors dimensionality reduction in deep learning — proving that stripping away all but one axis of control can create emergent depth from environmental complexity alone.
prototypes.notes: One-button endless runner. Procedural buildings with gaps and obstacles.

prototypes.name: cinematic-escape
prototypes.mechanic: cinematic platformer — zero HUD, zero dialogue, death-heavy trial-and-error; vector-animated alien world with environmental storytelling
prototypes.ancestry: Another World / Out of This World (1991, Éric Chahi, Delphine Software), Prince of Persia (1989, Jordan Mechner), Karateka (1984, Jordan Mechner)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — run/crouch | Space — jump | X — gun (tap shoot / hold shield / hold longer charge blast) | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3-5 (Knowledge Explosion to Deep Learning — single-developer cinematic vision, vector graphics as data-efficient representation, procedural animation)
prototypes.aiInsight: Éric Chahi's solo creation of Another World proved that a single developer with the right tools (rotoscoping, vector animation, a custom VM) could rival teams of dozens. This prefigures how AI-augmented solo creators can now build entire game worlds — the constraint of one mind forcing elegant, coherent design.
prototypes.notes: Zero HUD, zero dialogue, death as the primary teaching mechanism.

prototypes.name: reform-platform
prototypes.mechanic: shrinking platforms — jump between lily pads that shrink and sink after you land on them; collect items while platforms disappear beneath you
prototypes.ancestry: Perestroika (1990, Nikita Skripkin / Locis Software, USSR), Frogger (1981, Konami)
prototypes.controls: Left/Right arrows — move | Up/Space — jump | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Machine Learning — emergent difficulty through decaying platform states mirrors adaptive systems)
prototypes.aiInsight: Perestroika's shrinking platforms create an emergent difficulty curve where the game state itself degrades over time — a mechanic that mirrors entropy in complex adaptive systems and the concept of non-stationary environments in reinforcement learning.
prototypes.notes: Soviet-era political satire platformer. Lily pads shrink and sink after landing.

prototypes.name: morph-platform
prototypes.mechanic: deformable platformer — dash into soft platforms to reshape them; create ramps, indentations, and bridges by physically deforming the world
prototypes.ancestry: Semblance (2018, Nyamakop — Ben Myers & Cukia Kimani, Cape Town, South Africa)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move | Space — jump | Z — dash | R — reset deformations | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 6 (Generative AI — deformable worlds as player-authored generative design)
prototypes.aiInsight: Semblance inverts the platformer contract — instead of navigating fixed geometry, the player reshapes it. This mirrors generative AI's shift from consuming content to producing it; the world becomes a canvas for player expression.
prototypes.notes: Africa's indie breakout on Nintendo Switch. Soft platforms deform on dash impact.

prototypes.name: elastic-swing
prototypes.mechanic: rubber grapple physics — a fishing rod acts as an elastic grappling hook; swing, stretch, bounce off surfaces; the rubber physics create unique trajectories
prototypes.ancestry: Umihara Kawase (1994, TNN, Super Famicom, Japan cult classic) ← Bionic Commando (1987, Capcom) ← Pitfall! (1982, Activision)
prototypes.controls: Mouse aim + Click/Space — cast rod | A/D or Left/Right — move | W or Up — aim up | Release rod — fling | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Expert Systems — elastic physics simulation requires precise constraint solving and spring dynamics)
prototypes.aiInsight: Umihara Kawase's rubber-band grappling physics emerged from careful spring-damper simulation — the same differential equations that underpin modern physics engines and soft-body AI research.
prototypes.notes: Side-view platformer with elastic fishing-rod grappling hook. The rubber feel is everything —

prototypes.name: water-blast
prototypes.mechanic: water wave combat — throw water bombs that burst into waves flowing across platforms; water pools in gaps, flows downhill, and washes enemies away in cascades
prototypes.ancestry: Liquid Kids / Mizubaku Adventure (1990, Taito), Bubble Bobble (1986, Taito), Snow Bros (1990, Toaplan)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move left/right | Up — jump | Space — throw water bomb | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — fluid physics as game mechanic)
prototypes.aiInsight: Liquid Kids pioneered real-time fluid simulation as a core gameplay mechanic in 1990. The water bombs create emergent cascading behaviors — water flows downhill, pools in gaps, and sweeps enemies contextually. This represents an early attempt at physics-driven combat where the environment itself becomes the weapon, prefiguring modern physics sandbox games.
prototypes.notes: Water wave combat platformer. Throw water bombs that burst into flowing waves.

prototypes.name: bounce-throw
prototypes.mechanic: bouncing star physics — throw a star that bounces off walls and slopes following physics; ride the star as a platform; the star's trajectory depends on surface angles
prototypes.ancestry: Gimmick! (1992, Sunsoft) — Famicom/NES, released only in Japan and Scandinavia. One of the greatest games few people played.
prototypes.controls: Left/Right arrows — move | Space — jump | Z — throw star | R — restart | ? — info
