prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Machine Learning — the star's physics simulation reflects early computational approaches to real-time trajectory prediction)
prototypes.aiInsight: Gimmick!'s bouncing star mechanic is a real-time physics simulation that required players to internalize reflection angles — essentially becoming human trajectory predictors. The emergent puzzle-platforming created by physics interactions anticipated how ML systems learn to predict physical outcomes from observed trajectories.
prototypes.notes: Side-scrolling platformer with unique bouncing star mechanic.

prototypes.name: endless-dash
prototypes.mechanic: auto-running cookie — run automatically, jump over obstacles, slide under barriers, collect jellies; cookie has energy that depletes
prototypes.ancestry: Cookie Run (2013, Devsisters — Korea), Jetpack Joyride (2011, Halfbrick), Canabalt (2009, Adam Saltsman)
prototypes.controls: Space — jump (hold for higher) | Down — slide | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 6 (Foundation Models — massive mobile gaming era, global reach from Korean origins)
prototypes.aiInsight: Cookie Run's energy-depletion mechanic reframes the auto-runner from pure reflex into resource management — the player must balance risk (collecting distant jellies) against safety, mirroring exploration-exploitation tradeoffs central to reinforcement learning.
prototypes.notes: Korean auto-running cookie platformer. Devsisters (Korea, 2013) — massive in Korea and Asia.

prototypes.name: hex-strategy
prototypes.mechanic: slime possession — play as a slime that launches to stick to surfaces and possess host bodies; each host has different abilities; detach and re-possess to traverse levels
prototypes.ancestry: MO:Astray (2019, Archpray — Korea/Taiwan), Gish (2004, Chronic Logic), Flubby (experimental slime platformers)
prototypes.controls: Mouse aim + Click/Space — launch slime | Arrow keys — move host | Space — host action (shoot) | Z — detach from host | R — restart room | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 7 (Foundation Models — body-possession as context-switching mirrors multi-modal foundation model adaptation)
prototypes.aiInsight: MO:Astray's body-possession mechanic mirrors how foundation models adapt to different tasks by switching context. The slime is a general-purpose agent that gains specialized abilities through the bodies it inhabits — much like a single model fine-tuned for different domains.
prototypes.notes: Slime body-possession platformer. 4 rooms with puzzle-platformer

prototypes.name: rogue-dungeon
prototypes.mechanic: roguelite side-scrolling combat — fast-paced action with dash and dual-wield weapons; procedural dungeon rooms; shop between runs; multiple weapon types
prototypes.ancestry: Dungreed (2018, Team Horay — Korea), Dead Cells (2018, Motion Twin), Rogue Legacy (2013, Cellar Door Games)
prototypes.controls: ← → — move | ↑ — jump | Z — attack | X — dash | C — secondary weapon | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 7 (Foundation Models — procedural generation with emergent combat synergies)
prototypes.aiInsight: Dungreed's weapon-switching combat creates emergent combo systems from simple rule interactions — each weapon has distinct timing, range, and damage, and the player discovers optimal chains through experimentation. This mirrors how foundation models compose learned primitives into novel outputs.
prototypes.notes: Korean indie roguelite with fast-paced dash combat.

prototypes.name: columns
prototypes.mechanic: match-3 alignment — cycle jewel colors in a falling column of three
prototypes.ancestry: Columns (1990, Jay Geertsen/Sega), Bejeweled (2001, PopCap)
prototypes.controls: Left/Right or A/D — move | Up or W — cycle jewels | Down or S — fast drop | Space — instant drop | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 4 (Statistical Learning — pattern-based, pre-deep-learning)
prototypes.aiInsight: Columns is a constraint satisfaction problem over a 2D grid with falling pieces. Unlike Tetris (spatial packing), Columns is about color alignment — a different NP-hard problem. Match-3 became the foundation of a $10B mobile game category, proving that color matching is a universal cognitive primitive.
prototypes.notes: Different spatial logic than Tetris — alignment vs packing.

prototypes.name: incredible-machine
prototypes.mechanic: place machine parts to create chain-reaction physics solutions
prototypes.ancestry: The Incredible Machine (1993, Sierra/Jeff Tunnell), Rube Goldberg machines
prototypes.controls: Click — select/place parts | Arrow keys — rotate | Space — run/stop | R — reset | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 4 (Statistical Learning — physics simulation, constraint solving)
prototypes.aiInsight: The Incredible Machine is a constraint satisfaction problem in continuous physics space — the player must reason about how objects will interact under gravity and collision, placing components to create a valid chain from start to goal. This is planning in a physics simulator, a challenge that modern AI still struggles with.
prototypes.notes: The player IS the compiler — translating intent into physical chain reactions.

prototypes.name: pyramid-hop
prototypes.mechanic: hop on isometric pyramid, change tile colors to clear levels
prototypes.ancestry: Q*bert (1982, Warren Davis/Jeff Lee, Gottlieb), Marble Madness (1984, Atari)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — diagonal hop | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — spatial planning under threat)
prototypes.aiInsight: Q*bert's isometric grid creates a spatial coverage problem — visiting all tiles while avoiding enemies — structurally identical to the graph traversal problems that dominated 1980s AI planning research.
prototypes.notes: Isometric perspective adds visual complexity to simple grid movement.

prototypes.name: march-rescue
prototypes.mechanic: assign jobs to guide mindless walkers to exit safely
prototypes.ancestry: Lemmings (1991, DMA Design), Humans (1992, Imagitec Design)
prototypes.controls: Mouse — click lemming to assign job | 1-8 — select job type | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 4 (Statistical Learning — emergent behavior from simple agent rules)
prototypes.aiInsight: Lemmings are autonomous agents with minimal AI — they walk, turn at walls, and fall off edges. The player's role is essentially programming behavior modifications (dig, build, block) onto individual agents, making this one of the first mainstream agent-programming puzzles.
prototypes.notes: Lemmings walk mindlessly. Player assigns jobs to save them.

prototypes.name: rope-physics
prototypes.mechanic: cut ropes to swing object into target using gravity and physics
prototypes.ancestry: Cut the Rope (2010, ZeptoLab), The Incredible Machine (1993, Sierra), World of Goo (2008, 2D Boy)
prototypes.controls: Mouse — click and drag to cut ropes | R — restart level | N — next level | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 5 (Deep Learning — physics simulation as puzzle constraint)
prototypes.aiInsight: Cut the Rope proved that touch-native physics puzzles could achieve the depth of desktop games. Its rope simulation reduced complex physics to a single intuitive verb — cutting — making Newtonian mechanics accessible to anyone who can swipe a finger.
prototypes.notes: Rope physics puzzle with multiple solution paths per level.

prototypes.name: word-grid
prototypes.mechanic: crossword-style word placement on premium-square grid against AI
prototypes.ancestry: Scrabble (1948, Alfred Mosher Butts), Words with Friends (2009, Zynga), Literati (1996, Yahoo)
prototypes.controls: Mouse — select and place tiles | Enter — submit word | Backspace — recall tiles | Tab — exchange tiles | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — dictionary lookup, constraint satisfaction)
prototypes.aiInsight: Scrabble AI is a constraint satisfaction problem disguised as a word game. The AI must simultaneously satisfy lexical validity, board connectivity, and tile availability — a combinatorial search problem that early expert systems could solve but humans approach through vocabulary intuition.
prototypes.notes: Word game distilled to tile placement and vocabulary.

prototypes.name: pipedream
prototypes.mechanic: flow connection — place pipe segments before water arrives to create the longest possible path from the source
prototypes.ancestry: Pipe Mania (1989), Bioshock hacking (2007), Flow Free (2012)
prototypes.controls: Click — place pipe | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — graph traversal, flow networks)
prototypes.aiInsight: Pipe placement under time pressure is online graph construction — building a path in a partially observable environment before knowing what pieces you'll receive. This is the stochastic planning problem: act now with incomplete future information. The game teaches players to build robust partial plans.
prototypes.notes: Pipe Mania (1989) by The Assembly Line was later rebranded as Pipe Dream. The mechanic appears in countless games as a hacking/lockpicking minigame. The random piece queue adds a Tetris-like stochastic element.

prototypes.name: nonogram
prototypes.mechanic: picture logic — use row and column number clues to deduce which cells to fill, revealing a hidden pixel image
prototypes.ancestry: Nonograms (1987), Picross (1995 Nintendo), Paint It Back (2015)
prototypes.controls: Left click — fill cell | Right click — mark X | R — restart | N — next puzzle | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 4 (Expert Boom — constraint propagation, line solving)
prototypes.aiInsight: Nonogram solving is NP-complete in general, but most published puzzles are designed to be solvable by constraint propagation alone — line solving without backtracking. The puzzle teaches players systematic constraint reasoning: each row/column is an independent constraint that must agree with all orthogonal constraints.
prototypes.notes: Invented independently by Non Ishida (Japan) and Gwynneth Flower (UK) in 1987. Nintendo's Picross series made it mainstream. The hidden picture reveal provides a uniquely satisfying payoff for logical deduction.

prototypes.name: slidepuzzle
prototypes.mechanic: tile permutation — slide numbered tiles in a 4×4 grid with one empty space to reach the sorted configuration
prototypes.ancestry: 15-Puzzle (1874 Noyes Chapman), Sam Loyd's puzzle craze (1880), A* search heuristic
prototypes.controls: Click tile adjacent to empty — slide it | Arrow keys — slide tile into empty space | R — shuffle | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 1-5 (Search & Symbols → Statistical — A* search, Manhattan distance heuristic, IDA*)
prototypes.aiInsight: The 15-puzzle inspired fundamental search algorithms. The Manhattan distance heuristic (sum of each tile's distance from its goal) was one of the first admissible heuristics for A* search. Exactly half of all starting positions are unsolvable — determined by the parity of the permutation.
prototypes.notes: Caused a global puzzle craze in 1880. Sam Loyd offered $1000 for solving the 14-15 swap (impossible — wrong parity). The puzzle teaches permutation groups through play.

prototypes.name: tangram
prototypes.mechanic: shape fitting — arrange 7 geometric pieces (tans) to exactly fill a target silhouette shape
prototypes.ancestry: Tangram (~1800 China), dissection puzzles, spatial reasoning tests
prototypes.controls: Click+drag — move piece | R-click or scroll — rotate piece | F — flip piece | N — next puzzle | R — reset | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 5 (Statistical Learning — computer vision, geometric constraint satisfaction)
prototypes.aiInsight: Tangram is a geometric constraint satisfaction problem: 7 pieces must tile a region with zero gaps and zero overlap. This is equivalent to polygon packing — an NP-hard problem that computer vision and robotics solve for bin packing, nesting, and assembly planning.
prototypes.notes: The 7 tans (2 large triangles, 1 medium triangle, 2 small triangles, 1 square, 1 parallelogram) can form over 6500 different shapes. Universal across languages — no text needed. Used in cognitive psychology research on spatial reasoning.

prototypes.name: concentration
prototypes.mechanic: card memory matching — flip pairs of face-down cards to find matches; remember positions of previously seen cards
prototypes.ancestry: Concentration (~1700s parlor game), Pelmanism, Memory (Ravensburger 1959), TV game show (1958)
prototypes.controls: Click card — flip it | R — new game | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 2 (Expert Systems — associative memory, pattern matching)
prototypes.aiInsight: Concentration directly tests spatial working memory — the ability to bind objects to locations and recall them after interference. The game's difficulty scales with grid size in the same way that memory span experiments do. Human chunking strategies (grouping nearby cards) mirror the memory management strategies that cache architectures use.
prototypes.notes: The simplest possible memory game. Used extensively in cognitive science to study memory decay, interference, and age-related decline. Ravensburger's Memory (1959) sold 75+ million copies. The mechanic appears in countless video games as a mini-game.

prototypes.name: crossword
prototypes.mechanic: word clue grid — fill a grid with words from intersecting across and down clues
prototypes.ancestry: Arthur Wynne's Word-Cross (1913), New York Times Crossword (1942), Dr.Fill AI (2021)
prototypes.controls: Click cell — select | Type letter — fill | Arrow keys — navigate | Tab — next clue | Backspace — delete | R — new puzzle | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 5-7 (Statistical Learning → Generative-Agentic — NLP, constraint satisfaction, Dr.Fill 2021)
prototypes.aiInsight: Crossword construction is constraint satisfaction: each word must be valid AND intersecting letters must agree. Dr.Fill (Matt Ginsberg, 2021) used NLP embeddings to solve crosswords at human-expert level. The puzzle teaches vocabulary, lateral thinking, and constraint reasoning simultaneously.
prototypes.notes: Arthur Wynne published the first crossword in the New York World (1913). The NYT crossword (1942, edited by Will Shortz since 1993) is the gold standard. Crosswords are the most widely solved puzzle format in history.

prototypes.name: grow
prototypes.mechanic: combinatorial sequence — place items in order and watch them interact and level up; the order determines which items boost which; only one sequence is perfect
prototypes.ancestry: GROW (2002, On Nakayama / Eyezmaze), GROW RPG (2004), GROW Cube (2005)
prototypes.controls: Mouse — click items to place them on the planet | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 5 (Ubiquitous Intelligence — emergent interaction systems, combinatorial optimization)
prototypes.aiInsight: GROW is a combinatorial optimization puzzle where 8! = 40,320 permutations yield exactly one perfect solution. The interaction matrix creates a directed graph of dependencies — items boost others placed before them, creating chain reactions that reward systems thinking over brute force.
prototypes.notes: Combinatorial sequence puzzle distilled to item placement order.

prototypes.name: cursor10
prototypes.mechanic: time-clone cooperation — you have 10 cursors with a time limit; each cursor's clicks are recorded and replay as ghosts; cooperate with your past selves to solve puzzles
prototypes.ancestry: Cursor*10 (2008, Yoshio Ishii / Nekogames), Braid (2008, Jonathan Blow), The Misadventures of P.B. Winterbottom (2010)
prototypes.controls: Mouse — move and click | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 5 (Deep Learning — temporal replay systems, cooperative AI through self-play)
prototypes.aiInsight: Cursor*10 elegantly demonstrates self-cooperation through temporal replay — a concept that parallels multi-agent reinforcement learning where past policies become teammates. Each ghost cursor is essentially a frozen policy that the current agent must cooperate with, creating an emergent coordination problem.
prototypes.notes: Time-clone puzzle distilled to cursor cooperation across floors.

prototypes.name: color-match
prototypes.mechanic: pathfinding ball alignment — move colored balls on a 9×9 grid via clear paths; align 5+ of same color in a line to clear them; 3 new balls appear each turn if no line formed
prototypes.ancestry: Color Lines (1992, Olga Demina / Gennady Denisov / Igor Ivkin, Gamos Moscow), Winlines (1992), Lines 98 (1998, Gametop)
prototypes.controls: Mouse — click ball to select, click empty cell to move | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — pathfinding algorithms, BFS for reachability, combinatorial pattern matching)
prototypes.aiInsight: Color Lines is a pure planning puzzle wrapped in a pathfinding constraint. The BFS reachability check transforms a simple matching game into a spatial logistics problem — every move must consider not just alignment but accessibility, and the random ball spawns create an ever-tightening noose of blocked paths.
prototypes.notes: Russian puzzle classic distilled to grid pathfinding and color matching.

prototypes.name: farsh
prototypes.mechanic: carpet path rolling — roll out Persian carpets to create paths across puzzle levels; carpets can bridge gaps, cover hazards, stack; reach the exit
prototypes.ancestry: Farsh (2017, Mahdi Bahrami, Tehran), Sokoban (1982, Thinking Rabbit), Baba Is You (2019, Hempuli)
prototypes.controls: Mouse — click carpet then click direction arrow to unroll | WASD/Arrows — move character | R — restart level | N — next level (after win) | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 5 (Deep Learning — spatial reasoning for path construction under constraints)
prototypes.aiInsight: Farsh transforms the simple act of unrolling a carpet into a spatial reasoning puzzle. Each carpet is a rigid body with fixed length — the player must mentally simulate unrolling sequences to construct viable paths, a constraint-propagation problem that mirrors how neural networks learn spatial transformations.
prototypes.notes: Persian carpet aesthetic with rich reds, golds, and geometric border patterns.

prototypes.name: munin
prototypes.mechanic: level-section rotation — rotate rectangular sections of the level to create connected paths; the landscape shifts like puzzle pieces; you are Odin's raven seeking your wings
prototypes.ancestry: Munin (2014, Gojira — Sweden), Braid (2008), Tetris rotation, slide puzzles
prototypes.controls: Click section — rotate 90° clockwise | R — restart level | N — next level (after solving) | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 5 (Deep Learning — spatial reasoning, path planning through rotational transforms)
prototypes.aiInsight: Section rotation creates a combinatorial state space where each rotation changes connectivity. Unlike sliding puzzles where tiles translate, rotation fundamentally reshapes the topology — platforms become walls, gaps become bridges. The AI challenge is reasoning about how local rotations create global path connectivity.
prototypes.notes: Munin is Odin's memory-raven in Norse mythology.

prototypes.name: cincopaus
prototypes.mechanic: mystery wand roguelike — 5 wands with unknown random abilities; experiment to discover what each does; all text in Portuguese; deliberate obscurity as design
prototypes.ancestry: Cinco Paus (2017, Michael Brough), 868-HACK (2013, Michael Brough), Imbroglio (2016, Michael Brough)
prototypes.controls: Mouse — click wand then click direction to use | Arrow keys — move | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 7 (Generative Era — procedural mystery systems, emergent discovery through deliberate opacity)
prototypes.aiInsight: Cinco Paus inverts the traditional roguelike information paradigm. Where most games reward knowledge accumulation, Brough's design makes uncertainty the core mechanic. Each run generates fresh mysteries — the AI system that assigns abilities to wands creates a unique puzzle space where experimentation IS the gameplay, not preparation for it.
prototypes.notes: Mystery wand roguelike distilled to 7x7 grid experimentation.

prototypes.name: ball-maze
prototypes.mechanic: maze rotation — rotate the entire maze around a marble to guide it to the exit; you move the world not the ball
prototypes.ancestry: Cameltry (1989, Taito), Kororinpa: Marble Mania (2006, Hudson Soft), Super Monkey Ball (2001, Amusement Vision/Sega)
prototypes.controls: Left/Right arrows — rotate maze | R — restart level | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — physics simulation, real-time rotation transforms)
prototypes.aiInsight: Cameltry's genius is perceptual inversion. Instead of moving the player through the world, you rotate the world around the player. The marble obeys screen-space gravity while the maze rotates — a simple coordinate transform that creates deeply unintuitive yet learnable gameplay. The brain must decouple two reference frames simultaneously.
prototypes.notes: Rotating maze puzzle distilled to gravity and angular momentum.

prototypes.name: ring-shooter
prototypes.mechanic: chain shooting — shoot colored balls into a moving chain winding along a track; match 3+ to remove sections; prevent the chain from reaching the end
prototypes.ancestry: Puzz Loop (1998, Mitchell Corporation, Japan), Zuma (2003, PopCap Games), Luxor (2005, MumboJumbo)
prototypes.controls: Mouse — aim launcher | Click — shoot ball | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 5 (Deep Learning Dawn — neural pattern recognition, learned heuristics)
prototypes.aiInsight: Puzz Loop pioneered the marble-chain mechanic that Zuma popularized. The AI challenge is path prediction — calculating where a shot ball will intersect a moving chain on a curved track, requiring real-time trajectory and collision math that early arcade hardware handled through clever approximation.
prototypes.notes: Chain-shooting puzzle distilled to insertion and color matching.

prototypes.name: feed-match
prototypes.mechanic: animal-food matching — falling pairs of animals and food blocks; animals eat adjacent matching food in cascades; dog eats bones, rabbit eats carrots, panda eats bamboo, monkey eats bananas
prototypes.ancestry: Baku Baku Animal (1995, Sega), Puyo Puyo (1991, Compile), Columns (1990, Sega)
prototypes.controls: Left/Right or A/D — move pair | Up or W — rotate pair | Down or S — soft drop | Space — hard drop | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 4 (Statistical Learning — pattern matching and cascade evaluation)
prototypes.aiInsight: Baku Baku adds a predator-prey matching layer atop the falling-block formula. Instead of color matching, the AI must reason about typed relationships — animals eat specific foods. This asymmetric pairing creates a richer search space than simple color alignment, requiring the AI to track which block types enable future cascades.
prototypes.notes: The animal-food pairing mechanic is a twist on match-3 that adds semantic reasoning.

prototypes.name: drop-match
prototypes.mechanic: grab-stack-throw speed puzzle — walk beneath columns of colored drops; grab drops from above into your hands; throw them back up to create matches of 3+; the fastest competitive puzzle game
prototypes.ancestry: Magical Drop (1995, Data East), Magical Drop II (1996), Magical Drop III (1997), Puyo Puyo (1991, Compile), Puzzle Bobble (1994, Taito)
prototypes.controls: Left/Right — move character | Up/Space — grab or throw drops | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 4 (Expert Systems & Search — pattern recognition, combinatorial optimization)
prototypes.aiInsight: Magical Drop's grab-stack-throw mechanic creates an optimization problem unique among puzzle games. Unlike Tetris or Puyo Puyo where pieces arrive and must be placed, here the player actively harvests and redistributes existing pieces — a real-time constraint satisfaction problem where speed of recognition and execution dominates strategy.
prototypes.notes: Speed puzzle distilled to column-based grab-and-throw.

prototypes.name: swap-match
prototypes.mechanic: swap-match speed — swap adjacent blocks horizontally to create matches of 3+ same color; blocks rise from below; chain combos for huge scores; the purest competitive puzzle game
prototypes.ancestry: Panel de Pon (1995, Intelligent Systems / Nintendo), Tetris Attack (1995, Western localization), Pokemon Puzzle League (2000), Planet Puzzle League (2007)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move cursor | Space — swap blocks | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 4 (Heuristic Goldmine — pattern recognition, chain prediction, speed optimization)
prototypes.aiInsight: Panel de Pon's chain system creates an emergent complexity curve — setting up multi-chain combos requires spatial prediction and timing that rewards both human intuition and AI lookahead. The rising floor adds constant pressure that transforms a matching puzzle into a real-time strategy game.
prototypes.notes: Swap-match speed puzzle distilled to its purest form.

prototypes.name: star-click
prototypes.mechanic: hidden star discovery — each screen contains a star hidden by a unique interaction puzzle; click, drag, interact to discover and reveal it; every screen is different
prototypes.ancestry: Hoshi Saga (2007, Yoshio Ishii / Nekogames), Flash puzzle anthology, Cursor*10 (same creator)
prototypes.controls: Mouse — click/drag to interact | N — next screen | R — reset current screen | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 5 (Generalization Wave — each screen is a unique puzzle requiring different reasoning)
prototypes.aiInsight: Hoshi Saga is a meta-puzzle: the player must first discover WHAT to do before doing it. This mirrors the exploration-exploitation tradeoff in reinforcement learning — each screen demands a fresh hypothesis about the interaction model.
prototypes.notes: 12 unique puzzle screens, each hiding a star behind a different interaction.

prototypes.name: egg-roll
prototypes.mechanic: downward egg navigation — guide a penguin egg downward through platforms by breaking blocks beneath it; the egg rolls on slopes and cracks on long falls
prototypes.ancestry: Doki Doki Penguin Land (1985, Sega SG-1000/Master System, Japan)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move penguin | Space — break block penguin faces | R — restart level | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 2 (Knowledge-Based — rule-driven block physics and fragility simulation)
prototypes.aiInsight: Doki Doki Penguin Land inverted the platformer formula by making downward progress the goal, turning gravity from ally into threat. The egg's fragility creates a constraint-satisfaction puzzle where every block-break decision must account for cascading consequences — a design that presages modern physics puzzle games.
prototypes.notes: Downward puzzle-platformer with egg fragility mechanic.

prototypes.name: moneyexchanger
prototypes.mechanic: coin exchange arithmetic — coins fall; combine matching coins to exchange into higher denominations (five 1¥ = one 5¥, two 5¥ = one 10¥); clear 500¥ coins to score
prototypes.ancestry: Money Idol Exchanger (1997, Face, arcade/Neo Geo, Japan), Puzzle de Pon! (1995, Face)
prototypes.controls: Left/Right — move | Down — soft drop | Space — hard drop | Z — rotate pair | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 4 (Statistical Learning — pattern recognition and chain evaluation)
prototypes.aiInsight: Money Idol Exchanger turns arithmetic into spatial reasoning. The exchange rules mirror real currency — five 1s make a 5, two 5s make a 10 — so the mechanic is instantly intuitive to anyone who has handled money. The cascade potential (1¥ all the way to 500¥) creates a combinatorial explosion that rewards long-term planning over reactive play.
prototypes.notes: Coin arithmetic puzzle distilled to exchange chains.

prototypes.name: quizquiz
prototypes.mechanic: quiz puzzle hybrid — answer trivia questions; correct answers send puzzle blocks falling on opponent's field; fastest correct answer attacks most
prototypes.ancestry: QuizQuiz (1999, Nexon — Korea's first free-to-play online game), Puzzle Fighter (1996, Capcom), Quiz & Dragons (1992, Capcom)
prototypes.controls: 1-4 — answer A/B/C/D | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 5 (Connected Intelligence — online F2P model, competitive multiplayer quiz)
prototypes.aiInsight: QuizQuiz married trivia knowledge with competitive puzzle mechanics, creating a hybrid where intellectual speed becomes a weapon. Nexon's 1999 release pioneered the free-to-play business model that would reshape the entire games industry. The quiz-as-attack mechanic is a unique fusion — correct answers become offensive moves, transforming knowledge into competitive advantage.
prototypes.notes: Quiz-puzzle hybrid distilled to speed-knowledge-as-weapon.

prototypes.name: racer
prototypes.mechanic: perspective-road — pseudo-3D road races toward viewer, steer to survive, speed to win
prototypes.ancestry: Night Driver (1976, Atari), Pole Position (1982, Namco), Out Run (1986, Sega)
prototypes.controls: Left/Right arrows — steer | Up — accelerate | Down — brake | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 2 (Expert Systems — sprite scaling creates 3D illusion with 2D math, road curvature is pure trig)
prototypes.aiInsight: Pseudo-3D racing is one of gaming's great optical illusions. No 3D engine, no polygons — just scaled horizontal lines creating the sensation of depth. The road is a mathematical curve rendered as stacked trapezoids. Every racing game before polygon era lived inside this trick.
prototypes.notes: The perspective road illusion — one of gaming's most important visual inventions. Sprite scaling as proto-3D. Every racing game from Pole Position to Out Run to Road Rash used this technique.

prototypes.name: coast-racer
prototypes.mechanic: pseudo-3D racing on branching roads with traffic avoidance
prototypes.ancestry: Out Run (1986, Yu Suzuki, Sega), Pole Position (1982, Namco)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — steer/accelerate/brake | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — pseudo-3D rendering techniques)
prototypes.aiInsight: Out Run's road rendering uses a segment-based projection system where each horizontal line of the road is a transformed strip — a scanline-based approach that creates convincing 3D from pure 2D math. This geometric transformation is structurally related to the projection operations in early computer vision systems.
prototypes.notes: Branching paths give player agency in a traditionally linear genre.

prototypes.name: leap-racer
prototypes.mechanic: top-down driving with jumping over obstacles and water hazards
prototypes.ancestry: Bump 'n' Jump (1982, Data East), Spy Hunter (1983, Bally Midway)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — steer/accelerate/brake | Space — jump | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 2 (Expert Systems — scripted obstacle patterns)
prototypes.aiInsight: Bump 'n' Jump's dual mechanic of bumping cars off-road AND jumping over water creates a risk-reward decision at speed — a real-time resource allocation problem where the player must choose between offensive and defensive actions under time pressure.
prototypes.notes: Top-down vertical scroller. Drive forward, bump enemy cars off road.

prototypes.name: kart
prototypes.mechanic: kart racing with item pickups, drifting, and AI opponents on circuit
prototypes.ancestry: Super Mario Kart (1992, Nintendo), Micro Machines (1991, Codemasters), RC Pro-Am (1988, Rare)
prototypes.controls: Up — accelerate | Down — brake | Left/Right — steer | Shift — drift | Space — use item | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 4 (Statistical Learning — rubber-banding AI, item probability weighted by position)
prototypes.aiInsight: Mario Kart pioneered rubber-banding — AI that adapts difficulty based on player performance. This dynamic difficulty adjustment predated formal research into adaptive systems and remains one of gaming's most effective engagement mechanisms.
prototypes.notes: Kart racing distilled to its essentials: circuit, items, rivals.

prototypes.name: party-rpg
prototypes.mechanic: crawl dungeon in first person with party combat
prototypes.ancestry: The Bard's Tale (1985, Interplay/Michael Cranford), Wizardry (1981, Sir-Tech)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move/turn | Space — attack/select | 1-4 — select party member | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — procedural generation, growing game complexity)
prototypes.aiInsight: The dungeon crawl is a state-space search problem — the player explores a partially observable graph, building a mental model (map) through sequential observation. Party combat adds multi-agent coordination where each member's action must account for shared resources and complementary roles.
prototypes.notes: First-person perspective creates tension through limited visibility.

prototypes.name: ys
prototypes.mechanic: bump into enemies to fight — angle of approach matters
prototypes.ancestry: Ys: Ancient Ys Vanished (1987, Nihon Falcom), Hydlide (1984, T&E Soft)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move (bump enemies to attack) | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — action RPG emergence, real-time stat resolution)
prototypes.aiInsight: Ys eliminates the attack button entirely — combat IS movement. The player's approach angle determines damage, making spatial reasoning the only combat skill. This is a continuous action space reduced to a single verb (move), where positioning encodes all tactical decisions.
prototypes.notes: No attack button — movement IS combat. Angle of approach is everything.

prototypes.name: dungeon-crawl
prototypes.mechanic: top-down dungeon crawl with class selection, food timer, monster generators
prototypes.ancestry: Gauntlet (1985, Ed Logg, Atari), Dandy (1983, John Palevich)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move | Space — attack | 1-4 — class select | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — swarm AI and generator-based spawning)
prototypes.aiInsight: Gauntlet's monster generators create a producer-consumer dynamic — spawners produce threats faster than the player can eliminate them, forcing strategic prioritization of generators over individual enemies. This resource-pressure model appears in modern multi-agent swarm simulations.
prototypes.notes: Health-as-timer creates constant forward pressure.

prototypes.name: open-rpg
prototypes.mechanic: tile-based open world RPG with towns, dungeons, and turn-based combat
prototypes.ancestry: Ultima IV (1985, Richard Garriott, Origin Systems), Akalabeth (1979, Richard Garriott)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move | Space — interact/attack | I — inventory | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — NPC schedules and virtue systems)
prototypes.aiInsight: Ultima IV introduced a virtue system where the player's moral choices determined progression — the first game to use ethical behavior as a core mechanic. This value-alignment problem — ensuring the agent acts according to specified principles — is the central challenge of modern AI alignment research.
prototypes.notes: The open world creates a player-directed experience within systemic rules.

prototypes.name: post-apoc-rpg
prototypes.mechanic: post-apocalyptic party RPG with skill checks and tactical combat
prototypes.ancestry: Wasteland (1988, Interplay/Brian Fargo), The Bard's Tale (1985, Interplay)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move | Space — interact | A — attack | S — skill | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — skill-based outcome resolution)
prototypes.aiInsight: Wasteland's skill system used probabilistic outcome resolution based on character stats — an early stochastic model where success depended on hidden dice rolls modified by invested skill points. This probability-weighted decision system mirrors the utility functions in decision-theoretic AI planning.
prototypes.notes: Party-based exploration with permanent consequences was revolutionary for 1988.

prototypes.name: ascii-dungeon
prototypes.mechanic: turn-based roguelike with procedural dungeons, permadeath, and deep item interaction
prototypes.ancestry: NetHack (1987, NetHack Dev Team), Rogue (1980, Toy/Wichman), Hack (1985, Falkenthal)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys/WASD — move | Space — attack adjacent | I — inventory | G — pick up | D — drop | < > — stairs | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — procedural generation with complex rule systems)
prototypes.aiInsight: NetHack's procedural dungeon generation combined with its vast item interaction system creates emergent gameplay from deterministic rules — a combinatorial explosion of possibilities from simple components, presaging modern AI's interest in emergence from compositional systems.
prototypes.notes: Turn-based roguelike. Procedural dungeons. Permadeath. ASCII-style visuals on canvas.

prototypes.name: party-crawler
prototypes.mechanic: real-time first-person dungeon crawl with party management and grid movement
prototypes.ancestry: Dungeon Master (1987, FTL Games), Eye of the Beholder (1991, Westwood)
prototypes.controls: W/Up — forward | S/Down — backward | A — strafe left | D — strafe right | Q/E — turn | 1-4 — select character | Space — attack | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — real-time grid-based 3D with resource management)
prototypes.aiInsight: Dungeon Master pioneered real-time combat in first-person dungeon crawlers, replacing turn-based systems with time-pressure decision-making. The real-time element transforms tactical puzzle-solving into reactive resource management — a shift from deliberative to reactive AI paradigms.
prototypes.notes: First-person dungeon crawl on a grid. Party of 4 characters.

prototypes.name: dungeon-loot
prototypes.mechanic: click-to-move action RPG with procedural dungeons, loot, and leveling
prototypes.ancestry: Diablo (1997, Blizzard North), Rogue (1980, Toy/Wichman), Gauntlet (1985, Atari)
prototypes.controls: Click — move/attack | WASD — move | 1/2 — use potions | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 4 (Statistical Learning — procedural generation, loot probability tables)
prototypes.aiInsight: Diablo's procedural dungeon generation combined with randomized loot created a variable-ratio reinforcement schedule — the same psychological mechanism behind slot machines. This marriage of procedural content and reward psychology defined the action RPG genre.
prototypes.notes: Action RPG distilled to dungeon, loot, and level-up loop.

prototypes.name: time-crystal-rpg
prototypes.mechanic: time-split screen — the screen simultaneously shows past (left), present (center), and future (right); actions in the present change past and future in real-time; turn-based combat with time manipulation
prototypes.ancestry: Cris Tales (2021, Dreams Uncorporated / SYCK), Chrono Trigger (1995, Square), Paper Mario (2000, Intelligent Systems)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys / WASD — move | Space — interact | 1/2/3 — combat actions | Z/X — send to past/future | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 7 (Foundation Models — time-split visual storytelling, Colombian cultural design identity)
prototypes.aiInsight: Cris Tales' simultaneous past/present/future display is a spatial visualization of temporal branching — the player sees causality rendered in real-time across three panels. This UI innovation transforms time manipulation from an abstract mechanic into a visceral, visual experience. Colombia's first major international game.
prototypes.notes: Time-split screen with past (sepia), present (full color), future (blue tint).

prototypes.name: defender
prototypes.mechanic: horizontal scroll + protect — free horizontal scroll, shoot aliens, protect humans on ground
prototypes.ancestry: Defender (1981), Gradius (1985), R-Type (1987)
prototypes.controls: Left/Right — move | Up/Down — altitude | Space — shoot | Z — smart bomb | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — rule-based, pre-backpropagation)
prototypes.aiInsight: Invented the world-larger-than-screen problem — a playable POMDP in 1981. The minimap is dimensionality reduction: compressing full world state into an interpretable summary, exactly what autoencoders (1986) and attention mechanisms (2017) do.
prototypes.notes: Defender invented the free-scrolling world larger than the screen. The minimap is the second invention.

prototypes.name: scramble
prototypes.mechanic: forced-scroll shooter — dual weapons over terrain with fuel management
prototypes.ancestry: Scramble (1981, Konami), Super Cobra (1981, Konami), Gradius (1985, Konami)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys or WASD — move | Space — shoot | Z — bomb | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — rule-based, pre-backpropagation)
prototypes.aiInsight: First forced-scrolling shooter — the environment moves regardless of player input. This makes the game a real-time planning problem where future state is partially observable through the scrolling horizon. The dual weapon system (forward laser + downward bomb) creates a simple action space with positional context-dependent utility.
prototypes.notes: Scramble was the first forced-scrolling shooter — the world moves whether you're ready or not.

prototypes.name: thexder
prototypes.mechanic: transform between walking mech and flying jet with auto-aim laser
prototypes.ancestry: Thexder (1985, Game Arts), Robotech/Macross games
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move | Space — transform mech/jet | Z — shoot laser | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — mode switching, adaptive control schemes)
prototypes.aiInsight: Thexder's dual-mode design is a policy-switching problem — the player maintains two distinct control policies (walk vs. fly) and must select the appropriate one based on environmental context. The auto-aim laser in mech mode is an early example of assisted targeting, presaging modern aim-assist AI.
prototypes.notes: Transformation is the core mechanic — environment dictates which form to use.

prototypes.name: ikari
prototypes.mechanic: scroll upward through enemy territory, shoot in eight directions
prototypes.ancestry: Ikari Warriors (1986, SNK), Commando (1985, Capcom)
prototypes.controls: WASD — move | Arrow keys — aim/shoot | Space — grenade | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — independent move/aim axes, increasing game complexity)
prototypes.aiInsight: Ikari Warriors separates movement from aiming — the player manages two independent control vectors simultaneously. This dual-output control problem mirrors multi-headed neural networks where separate output streams must coordinate toward a common objective.
prototypes.notes: Independent move and aim creates cognitive load that IS the challenge.

prototypes.name: warbird-scroll
prototypes.mechanic: vertical scrolling WWII dogfight with loop roll evasion
prototypes.ancestry: 1942 (1984, Yoshiki Okamoto, Capcom), Xevious (1982, Namco)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move | Space — shoot | Z — loop roll | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — formation-based enemy patterns)
prototypes.aiInsight: 1942's enemy formations follow scripted choreography — predetermined flight paths that create pattern-recognition challenges. This scripted-path approach to NPC behavior remains the foundation of shmup design and influenced waypoint-based AI navigation.
prototypes.notes: The loop roll mechanic adds a defensive option to pure-offense shooting.

prototypes.name: iso-shooter
prototypes.mechanic: isometric scrolling with altitude management through fortress
prototypes.ancestry: Zaxxon (1982, Ikegami Tsushinki, Sega), Buck Rogers: Planet of Zoom (1982, Sega)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move/altitude | Space — shoot | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — pseudo-3D spatial reasoning)
prototypes.aiInsight: Zaxxon was the first arcade game to use axonometric projection, forcing players to reason about a 2.5D space using a shadow as altitude reference. This spatial-to-visual mapping problem — inferring 3D from 2D cues — is structurally identical to depth estimation challenges in computer vision.
prototypes.notes: Altitude shadow is the key UI innovation — the only reliable height indicator.

prototypes.name: space-dogfight
prototypes.mechanic: space combat dogfight from cockpit view with radar and targeting
prototypes.ancestry: Wing Commander (1990, Chris Roberts, Origin Systems), Star Wars (1983, Atari)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — pitch/yaw | Space — fire lasers | M — fire missile | T — target next | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — cinematic AI with scripted behaviors and state machines)
prototypes.aiInsight: Wing Commander's enemy fighters used behavior trees with personality parameters — some enemies would flee when damaged, others would kamikaze. Combined with wingman AI that responded to commands, it created one of gaming's first believable multi-agent combat simulations.
prototypes.notes: Cockpit-view space shooter. Target and destroy enemy fighters.

prototypes.name: run-and-gun
prototypes.mechanic: side-scrolling run-and-gun with 8-directional shooting and power-up weapons
prototypes.ancestry: Contra (1987, Konami), Gryzor (1987, Konami arcade), Metal Slug (1996, SNK)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move/aim | Space — shoot | J — jump | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — pattern-based enemy waves and boss choreography)
prototypes.aiInsight: Contra's enemy spawning uses carefully choreographed patterns — soldiers run in from specific directions, turrets fire at timed intervals, and bosses have phased attack sequences. This scripted choreography creates the illusion of intelligent opposition through precise timing design.
prototypes.notes: Side-scrolling run-and-gun. One-hit death. Power-up weapons.

prototypes.name: tube-shooter
prototypes.mechanic: tube-rim abstract shooter — move along the rim of geometric shapes and shoot inward at enemies climbing toward you; vector-style graphics
prototypes.ancestry: Tempest (1981, Dave Theurer, Atari)
prototypes.controls: Left/Right or A/D — move along rim | Space or Z — shoot | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 2 (Discrete Logic — hardware-driven vector rendering and lane-based movement)
prototypes.aiInsight: Tempest's lane-based movement is a clever discrete spatial representation — the continuous tube is quantized into segments, reducing the movement problem to a 1D array. This discretization makes enemy AI trivial (climb your lane) while creating emergent complexity through lane-switching Flippers.
prototypes.notes: Vector-style neon graphics on black. Tube shapes change per level.

prototypes.name: vertical-descent
prototypes.mechanic: vertical gunboot shooter — fall down a procedural well; gun boots shoot downward to destroy enemies and slow descent; stomping enemies reloads ammo; red/white/black only
prototypes.ancestry: Downwell (2015, Ojiro Fumoto), Spelunky (2008, Derek Yu), Cave Story (2004, Daisuke Amaya)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move left/right | Space — fire gun boots downward | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 6 (Modern ML — procedural generation with learned layout patterns and adaptive difficulty)
prototypes.aiInsight: Downwell's procedural well generation uses hand-authored room templates stitched together with random selection, creating readable yet surprising vertical layouts. The combo system — consecutive kills without landing — creates emergent risk/reward: stay airborne longer for bigger combos but risk running out of ammo and hitting enemies from the side.
prototypes.notes: Vertical gunboot roguelike. Fall down a procedural well.

prototypes.name: swarm-rescue
prototypes.mechanic: twin-stick — move and shoot independently in all directions, wave-based arena survival
prototypes.ancestry: Robotron: 2084 (1982, Williams/Eugene Jarvis), Smash TV (1990), Geometry Wars (2003)
prototypes.controls: WASD — move | Arrow keys — shoot direction | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Early Neural — enemy wave choreography, rescue mechanics, escalating threat matrices)
prototypes.aiInsight: Twin-stick decoupled movement from attack for the first time. Before Robotron, you moved toward danger to fight it. After Robotron, you could flee and fight simultaneously. This single mechanical innovation birthed an entire genre and changed action game design forever. The family rescue mechanic added emotional stakes to pure survival.
prototypes.notes: Eugene Jarvis's masterpiece. The twin-stick mechanic is one of gaming's most consequential inventions — it split the player's attention into two parallel control streams, creating cognitive load as game design.

prototypes.name: room-escape
prototypes.mechanic: room-by-room arena — shoot robots, avoid walls, flee Evil Otto
prototypes.ancestry: Berzerk (1980, Stern Electronics), Frenzy (1982, Stern)
prototypes.controls: WASD — move | Arrow keys — shoot direction | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 2 (Expert Systems — rule-based, no learning)
prototypes.aiInsight: Evil Otto is an unkillable pursuer with simple deterministic behavior — it bounces and chases. This creates asymmetric threat levels: robots are killable puzzles, Otto is a hard timer. The combination of solvable and unsolvable threats in the same space creates a planning problem where the player must triage — a concept formalized in multi-objective optimization decades later.
prototypes.notes: Room transitions make each screen a self-contained combat puzzle.

prototypes.name: biplanes
prototypes.mechanic: fly biplane dogfight with gravity, thrust, and screen wrap
prototypes.ancestry: Biplanes (1982, Intellivision/Mattel), Combat (1977, Atari)
prototypes.controls: ← → — rotate | ↑ — thrust | Space — shoot | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 2 (Expert Systems — simple rule-based opponent behavior)
prototypes.aiInsight: The biplane dogfight adds gravity to the rotation-thrust model of Asteroids, creating a richer state space where altitude is a depletable resource. The AI opponent uses simple heuristics (turn toward player, maintain altitude) — demonstrating that rule-based agents can create engaging adversaries when physics provides the complexity.
prototypes.notes: Gravity transforms Asteroids-style controls into a different game.

prototypes.name: corridor-shooter
prototypes.mechanic: first-person raycasting shooter with key-locked progression
prototypes.ancestry: Doom (1993, id Software), Wolfenstein 3D (1992, id Software)
prototypes.controls: W/Up — forward | S/Down — backward | A — strafe left | D — strafe right | Left/Right — turn | Space — shoot | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — raycasting as real-time 3D approximation)
prototypes.aiInsight: Doom's raycasting engine solved the 3D rendering problem by reducing it to 2D — casting rays from the player's viewpoint to determine wall distances. This dimensional reduction technique mirrors approaches in machine learning where high-dimensional problems are projected into lower dimensions for tractability.
prototypes.notes: Raycasting first-person shooter. Navigate corridors, find keys, shoot enemies.

prototypes.name: royale
prototypes.mechanic: last-standing arena shooter with shrinking safe zone and loot
prototypes.ancestry: PlayerUnknown's Battlegrounds (2017, PUBG Corp), Fortnite (2017, Epic), H1Z1 (2015, Daybreak)
prototypes.controls: WASD — move | Mouse — aim | Click — shoot | E — pickup | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 6 (Transformer Era — emergent gameplay from simple rules at scale)
prototypes.aiInsight: Battle royale proved that a shrinking boundary was sufficient to force engagement in a large-scale game. This single spatial constraint — reducing the possibility space over time — solved the camping problem that plagued earlier multiplayer shooters and created guaranteed dramatic convergence.
prototypes.notes: Battle royale distilled: shrinking zone forces confrontation.

prototypes.name: tactical
prototypes.mechanic: top-down tactical shooter with cover, weapon switching, and room clearing
prototypes.ancestry: Call of Duty (2003, Infinity Ward), Medal of Honor (1999, DreamWorks), Counter-Strike (2000, Valve)
prototypes.controls: WASD — move | Mouse — aim/shoot | 1/2/3 — weapons | R — reload | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 5 (Deep Learning — enemy AI uses cover-seeking, flanking, suppression behaviors)
prototypes.aiInsight: Modern military shooters elevated enemy AI from simple patrol-and-chase to tactical behavior — seeking cover, flanking, coordinating attacks. This mirrors the shift from reactive to planning-based AI, where agents reason about spatial relationships and opponent state.
prototypes.notes: Tactical shooter stripped to essential loop: advance, engage, clear.

prototypes.name: cell-absorb
prototypes.mechanic: cell growth arena — eat smaller cells and food pellets to grow; avoid larger cells; split yourself to attack; massively multiplayer minimalism with AI opponents
prototypes.ancestry: Agar.io (2015, Matheus Valadares)
prototypes.controls: Mouse — move toward cursor | WASD — move | Space — split | W — eject mass | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 7 (Self-Play & Emergent — emergent predator/prey dynamics from simple size-based rules)
prototypes.aiInsight: Agar.io demonstrated that profound emergent complexity arises from trivial rules: bigger eats smaller. No scripted alliances, no designed strategies — yet players spontaneously developed teaming, splitting attacks, virus usage, and bait tactics. The game is a petri dish for emergent behavior, mirroring how simple reward signals in AI produce complex strategies.
prototypes.notes: Cell growth arena distilled to eat-or-be-eaten loop.

prototypes.name: arc-artillery
prototypes.mechanic: mobile artillery — choose a vehicle (mobile), aim angle + power with wind; each mobile has unique special shot; Worms-like with vehicle RPG elements
prototypes.ancestry: GunBound (2003, Softnyx), Worms (1995, Team17), Scorched Earth (1991, Wendell Hicken)
prototypes.controls: Up/Down — aim angle | Space (hold) — charge power | S — special shot | Left/Right — move | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 5 (Deep Learning — AI opponent calculates ballistic trajectories with wind compensation, mobile-specific shot selection)
prototypes.aiInsight: GunBound's vehicle selection meta-game layers strategic identity onto artillery physics. Each mobile creates a distinct problem space — the same wind and terrain demand different solutions depending on your vehicle. This mirrors ensemble methods in ML where different model architectures excel at different problem domains.
prototypes.notes: Korean mobile artillery (Softnyx, 2003 — 110 million registered users).

prototypes.name: trade-voyage
prototypes.mechanic: sail between ports trading goods for profit under risk
prototypes.ancestry: Taipan! (1982, Art Canfil), Lemonade Stand (1979, MECC)
prototypes.controls: 1-5 — menu options | ← → — select goods | Enter — confirm | Space — fire (combat) | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 2 (Expert Systems — economic modeling, risk assessment)
prototypes.aiInsight: Taipan is a sequential decision problem under uncertainty — the player optimizes a buy-sell strategy across a stochastic price graph with risk events. This is the multi-armed bandit problem applied to spatial economics, where each port is an arm with non-stationary rewards and the travel cost is the exploration penalty.
prototypes.notes: Buy low sell high is the simplest economic insight, yet endlessly engaging.

prototypes.name: utopia
prototypes.mechanic: build islands, manage resources, grow population in real-time
prototypes.ancestry: Utopia (1982, Don Daglow, Mattel/Intellivision), SimCity (1989, Maxis)
prototypes.controls: Mouse — select/place buildings | 1-5 — select building type | Space — pause | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — expert systems applied to simulation)
prototypes.aiInsight: Utopia pioneered real-time resource management as gameplay — balancing food, gold, and population growth mirrors multi-variable optimization problems that expert systems were beginning to tackle in the early 1980s.
prototypes.notes: Real-time god game. Place buildings on an island grid to grow population.

prototypes.name: frontier-trek
prototypes.mechanic: resource management journey with random events and survival decisions
prototypes.ancestry: The Oregon Trail (1971/1985, Don Rawitsch, MECC), The Yukon Trail (1994, MECC)
prototypes.controls: 1-5 — menu choices | Space — continue | H — hunt | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — Monte Carlo simulation of journey outcomes)
prototypes.aiInsight: Oregon Trail pioneered educational simulation — using random event generation and resource depletion models to teach decision-making under uncertainty. The game's event system is essentially a simplified Monte Carlo simulation where each day samples from probability distributions.
prototypes.notes: Travel from Independence to Oregon. Manage food, health, pace.

prototypes.name: empire-builder
prototypes.mechanic: 4X turn-based strategy — explore, expand, exploit, exterminate on a world map
prototypes.ancestry: Civilization (1991, Sid Meier, MicroProse), Empire (1977, Walter Bright)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys/Mouse — scroll map | Click — select/move unit | B — build city | T — end turn | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 4 (Statistical Learning — multi-objective AI opponents with economic modeling)
prototypes.aiInsight: Civilization's AI must balance exploration, expansion, economic development, military production, and diplomacy simultaneously — a multi-objective optimization problem that mirrors the challenges of real-world resource allocation that operations research had been studying for decades.
prototypes.notes: Turn-based 4X strategy. Build cities, research tech, train units.

prototypes.name: god-sim
prototypes.mechanic: god game — terraform land and guide followers to dominate opposing tribe
prototypes.ancestry: Populous (1989, Peter Molyneux, Bullfrog), Powermonger (1990, Bullfrog)
prototypes.controls: Mouse — raise/lower terrain, select powers | 1-4 — select divine power | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — agent-based population simulation)
prototypes.aiInsight: Populous pioneered the god game genre where the player influences rather than directly controls — followers are autonomous agents that respond to environmental changes. This indirect control paradigm mirrors principal-agent problems in economics and multi-agent AI systems.
prototypes.notes: God game. Raise/lower terrain to help followers build and expand.

prototypes.name: lane-battle
prototypes.mechanic: lane-based real-time card battler with elixir economy and auto-battling units
prototypes.ancestry: Clash Royale (2016, Supercell), Defense of the Ancients (2003, Eul), Herzog Zwei (1989, Technosoft)
prototypes.controls: Mouse — select card and deploy | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 6 (Transformer Era — real-time resource management, unit composition strategy)
prototypes.aiInsight: Clash Royale compressed the MOBA formula into 3-minute matches by making units autonomous. The player's only decisions are what to deploy, where, and when — reducing real-time strategy to pure resource management and timing. This abstraction made deep strategy accessible on mobile.
prototypes.notes: MOBA meets card game in miniature. Elixir economy forces constant

prototypes.name: village-raid
prototypes.mechanic: base building defense and army deployment attack in alternating phases
prototypes.ancestry: Clash of Clans (2012, Supercell), Tower Defense genre (2000s), Rampart (1990, Atari)
prototypes.controls: Mouse — place buildings/deploy troops | Tab — switch phase | 1-4 — select unit type | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 6 (Transformer Era — base layout optimization, troop deployment AI)
prototypes.aiInsight: Clash of Clans split strategy into two asymmetric problems: defense design (offline optimization) and attack execution (real-time deployment). This separation mirrors the train/inference split in machine learning — one phase optimizes a static structure, the other executes decisions against it.
prototypes.notes: Build-then-attack loop creates two distinct strategy layers.

prototypes.name: tactics
prototypes.mechanic: turn-based grid tactics with unit types, terrain bonuses, and territory capture
prototypes.ancestry: Great Little War Game (2011, Rubicon), Advance Wars (2001, Intelligent Systems), Battle Isle (1991, Blue Byte)
prototypes.controls: Mouse — select units, move, attack | Enter — end turn | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 4 (Statistical Learning — minimax-style tactical evaluation, positional play)
prototypes.aiInsight: Turn-based tactics games are discrete optimization problems where terrain transforms the payoff matrix. The AI must evaluate not just unit matchups but positional advantage — a spatial reasoning challenge that connects to classical game tree search with evaluation functions informed by domain knowledge.
prototypes.notes: Turn-based tactics stripped to core: move, attack, capture.

prototypes.name: farmstead
prototypes.mechanic: farming cycle — till soil, plant seeds, water crops, harvest produce, sell for profit, and expand your farm through seasonal cycles
prototypes.ancestry: Harvest Moon (1996), FarmVille (2009), Stardew Valley (2016)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys or WASD — move | 1-4 — select tool (hoe/seeds/water/harvest) | E — sell at market | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 6 (Deep Learning — optimization loops, reinforcement learning)
prototypes.aiInsight: Farming games encode a multi-objective optimization problem: maximize profit while managing time, energy, crop rotation, and seasonal constraints. The satisfying loop of invest→wait→harvest→reinvest mirrors the explore-exploit tradeoff in reinforcement learning. Stardew Valley proved that this loop, combined with social simulation, creates one of gaming's most compelling retention mechanisms.
prototypes.notes: Harvest Moon (1996) by Yasuhiro Wada created the farming sim genre. Stardew Valley (2016) by one developer (ConcernedApe) became a cultural phenomenon. The core loop — plant, tend, harvest, sell, upgrade — is a perfect economic feedback loop.

prototypes.name: idle
prototypes.mechanic: incremental growth — click to generate resources, buy upgrades that produce resources automatically, unlocking exponential growth loops
prototypes.ancestry: Progress Quest (2002), Cookie Clicker (2013), Universal Paperclips (2017), Antimatter Dimensions (2017)
prototypes.controls: Click — generate resource | Click buttons — buy upgrades | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 6 (Deep Learning — exponential scaling, reward shaping)
prototypes.aiInsight: Idle games are pure reward-shaping experiments. The exponential growth curve with diminishing returns per upgrade models the same multi-armed bandit problem that RL agents face. Cookie Clicker's prestige system is a meta-learning reset — sacrifice current progress for a permanent multiplier, mirroring transfer learning across training runs.
prototypes.notes: Cookie Clicker (2013) by Orteil proved that the act of watching numbers go up is inherently compelling. The genre strips away all traditional game design (skill, challenge, narrative) to expose the pure psychology of progress and optimization.

prototypes.name: typeracer
prototypes.mechanic: motor speed measurement — type displayed text as fast and accurately as possible; WPM and accuracy scoring
prototypes.ancestry: Mavis Beacon Teaches Typing (1987), TypeRacer (2008), ZType (2011), Monkeytype (2020)
prototypes.controls: Type displayed text | Enter — start/next passage | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 1-7 (Search & Symbols → Generative-Agentic — human-computer interaction, motor learning, input bandwidth)
prototypes.aiInsight: Touch typing is the most practiced motor skill in computing. WPM measures the bandwidth of human text output — typically 40-80 WPM for proficient typists, 150+ for champions. This bandwidth constraint fundamentally shapes how humans interact with AI systems. The game measures the bottleneck in human-computer communication.
prototypes.notes: Mavis Beacon (1987) made typing practice into a game. TypeRacer (2008) added competitive multiplayer. Monkeytype (2020) became the modern standard. The typing test is simultaneously a game, a skill assessment, and a measurement of human-computer interface throughput.

prototypes.name: mule
prototypes.mechanic: economic colonization — claim land plots, deploy mules to harvest food/energy/ore, trade in real-time auctions with supply/demand pricing
prototypes.ancestry: M.U.L.E. (1983, Ozark Softscape / Electronic Arts), creator Dani Bunten Berry
prototypes.controls: Mouse — click to select plots, resources, auction actions | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Expert Systems — supply/demand pricing, resource optimization heuristics)
prototypes.aiInsight: M.U.L.E. is a landmark in multiplayer economic game design. Its real-time auction mechanic — where buyers walk down and sellers walk up until they meet — elegantly encodes supply and demand into physical movement. Dani Bunten Berry pioneered cooperative-competitive design where player interaction IS the game, not combat overlaid on it. The AI opponents must balance resource production, cash reserves, and auction strategy — a multi-objective optimization problem that early expert systems approached with hand-tuned heuristics.
prototypes.notes: Simplified colony management with the iconic auction mechanic intact.

prototypes.name: virtualpet
prototypes.mechanic: virtual life simulation — watch a tiny person live in a cross-section house; interact via gifts, petting, and commands; no win condition
prototypes.ancestry: Little Computer People (1985, Activision), Tamagotchi (1996, Bandai), The Sims (2000, Maxis)
prototypes.controls: Mouse — click character to pet, click objects to interact | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3-7 (Rule Systems through Modern AI — from early autonomous agents to sophisticated virtual life simulation)
prototypes.aiInsight: Little Computer People was one of the first games where AI characters exhibited autonomous behavior — making their own decisions based on internal needs. This approach presaged modern virtual pet AI and The Sims' need-driven autonomy, where agents prioritize actions based on weighted utility functions.
prototypes.notes: Virtual pet stripped to core: autonomous character with needs, cross-section house, player interaction.

prototypes.name: drill-mine
prototypes.mechanic: downward mining — drill through dirt on Mars to collect minerals; manage fuel and hull integrity; sell minerals to upgrade drill; deeper means rarer minerals and greater danger
prototypes.ancestry: Motherload (2004, XGen Studios), Dig Dug (1982, Namco), Mr. Driller (1999, Namco), SteamWorld Dig (2013, Image & Form)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move/drill | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 5 (Deep Learning — procedural mineral distribution, risk/reward depth curves)
prototypes.aiInsight: Motherload distills the core loop of resource extraction games into its purest form — the downward journey as metaphor for increasing commitment. Each descent is a gamble: fuel is finite, minerals are uncertain, and the surface grows farther away. The game teaches sunk-cost reasoning through embodied experience.
prototypes.notes: Downward mining with upgrade progression loop.

prototypes.name: unbox-sort
prototypes.mechanic: object placement storytelling — unpack boxes into rooms; each item has a correct spot; no instructions; the story emerges from what items appear across moves
prototypes.ancestry: Unpacking (2021, Witch Beam — Brisbane, Australia), A Short Hike (2019, adamgryu), Florence (2018, Mountains)
prototypes.controls: Mouse — click box to unpack, drag items to place | R — restart room | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 7 (Foundation Models — zen narrative design, environmental storytelling through object arrangement)
prototypes.aiInsight: Unpacking inverts game design conventions by removing instructions, scores, and timers. The player discovers meaning through the spatial grammar of everyday objects. Each room is a puzzle whose solution is empathy — understanding where a person would put their things. This is procedural rhetoric through placement, a design philosophy that AI systems can learn from: sometimes the most powerful interaction is the gentlest.
prototypes.notes: Zen object-placement across three life stages.

prototypes.name: denshadego
prototypes.mechanic: train driving precision — operate a Japanese commuter train; manage speed and braking to stop precisely at station platforms; the pride of Japanese rail precision
prototypes.ancestry: Densha de Go! (1996, Taito), Train Simulator (2001, Dovetail), BVE Trainsim (1996–2011, mackoy)
prototypes.controls: Up — throttle | Down — brake | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 4 (Statistical Learning — precision control loops, timing schedules, spatial stopping algorithms)
prototypes.aiInsight: Train driving simulators are constrained optimization problems: arrive on time, stop precisely, obey speed limits. The tension between schedule pressure and braking physics creates a satisfying control loop where mastery means internalizing the dynamics model — the same challenge faced by real-time control systems.
prototypes.notes: Densha de Go! distilled to its essence: throttle, brake, precision stop.

prototypes.name: recettear
prototypes.mechanic: item shop economics — buy items from adventurers, set prices, sell to customers; go dungeon-crawling to find rare stock; capitalism ho!
prototypes.ancestry: Recettear: An Item Shop's Tale (2007, EasyGameStation, doujin, Japan)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move (dungeon) | Space — attack (dungeon) | Click — select items, adjust prices (shop) | N — next day | D — go to dungeon | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 6 (Foundation Models — shop economics as supply/demand optimization, NPC haggling as negotiation agents)
prototypes.aiInsight: Running a shop inverts the typical RPG perspective — instead of consuming items, you must understand their perceived value to NPCs. The haggling mechanic is a repeated negotiation game where pricing signals encode information about customer budgets and preferences, a microcosm of market dynamics that modern AI explores through multi-agent economic simulations.
prototypes.notes: Two-phase loop: shop management and dungeon crawling.

prototypes.name: territory-dice
prototypes.mechanic: dice territory conquest — Risk-like territory control on a hex grid; each territory has stacked dice; attack adjacent territory by rolling all dice, higher total wins; fast 5-minute games
prototypes.ancestry: Risk (1957, Albert Lamorisse), Dicewars (2005, Taro Ito, Flash, Japan)
prototypes.controls: Mouse — click your territory then click adjacent enemy to attack | End Turn button | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 5 (Deep Learning — probabilistic attack evaluation, greedy expansion with connectivity awareness)
prototypes.aiInsight: Dicewars reduces territorial strategy to pure probability. Each attack is a known distribution (sum of N dice vs sum of M dice). The optimal strategy balances risk-taking with preserving connected territory for bonus dice. AI must evaluate not just immediate win probability but downstream effects on territory connectivity — a graph theory problem wrapped in probability.
prototypes.notes: Hex grid territory control with dice stacking. Elegant information display — dice count IS army strength. Random map generation for replayability. 4-player free-for-all.

prototypes.name: windrunner
prototypes.mechanic: wind-powered endless runner — run and jump on the ground, then wind gusts lift you into gliding sky sections; transition between ground running and sky gliding is the signature feel
prototypes.ancestry: Wind Runner (2013, Actoz Soft / Korea), Temple Run (2011, Imangi), Jetpack Joyride (2011, Halfbrick), Canabalt (2009, Semi Secret)
prototypes.controls: Space — jump (ground) / flap (sky) | Up/Down — glide up/down (sky) | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 6 (Foundation Models — procedural generation of dual-mode terrain with smooth transitions between running and flying states)
prototypes.aiInsight: Wind Runner's dual-mode design creates a fascinating state-space bifurcation — the same player input maps to fundamentally different physics depending on ground vs sky mode. This mirrors how foundation models learn context-dependent representations where identical tokens carry different meaning based on surrounding context.
prototypes.notes: Korean endless runner with signature wind-lift mechanic.

prototypes.name: kingdomwinds
prototypes.mechanic: Korean MMO origin — top-down RPG with real-time combat; Korea's first graphical online game; based on Korean manhwa Kingdom of the Winds by Kim Jin
prototypes.ancestry: The Kingdom of the Winds (1996, Nexon/Jake Song), Ultima Online (1997, Origin), Lineage (1998, NCsoft/Jake Song)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move | Space — attack | E — interact/talk | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 4 (Statistical Learning — NPC pathfinding, spawn logic, quest state machines)
prototypes.aiInsight: The Kingdom of the Winds pioneered the Korean MMO model that would dominate Asian gaming for decades. Jake Song's design married single-player RPG depth with persistent multiplayer worlds. The quest-kill-loot-return loop became the foundational grammar of online RPGs worldwide. This prototype distills that loop into its purest form.
prototypes.notes: Korea's first graphical online game (1996), created by Jake Song at Nexon.

prototypes.name: turnbased-rpg
prototypes.mechanic: hex-grid SRPG — move units on hex tiles, attack adjacent enemies, turn-based tactical combat with terrain bonuses; Korea's first original RPG
prototypes.ancestry: Astonishia Story (1994, Sonnori) — Korea's first RPG developer
prototypes.controls: Mouse — select unit, click hex to move/attack | Enter — end turn | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Expert Systems — rule-based tactical AI with terrain evaluation and threat assessment)
prototypes.aiInsight: Hex grids create six-directional adjacency versus four for square grids, expanding tactical possibility space. The AI evaluates terrain bonuses and threat zones using heuristic rules — an expert system approach that mirrors how human players learn tactical principles through experience.
prototypes.notes: Korea's first SRPG, Sonnori's founding game. Hex grid for richer positioning.

prototypes.name: minigolf
prototypes.mechanic: aim and putt ball through obstacle courses into hole
prototypes.ancestry: Zany Golf (1988, Electronic Arts), Mini-Putt (various 1980s+)
prototypes.controls: Click+drag on ball — aim and set power | R — restart hole | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — physics simulation in games matures)
prototypes.aiInsight: Mini golf is a trajectory planning problem with imperfect execution — the player must simulate ball physics mentally, accounting for reflections and friction. Each shot is a hypothesis about the physical world that is immediately tested. This predict-then-observe loop mirrors model-based reinforcement learning.
prototypes.notes: Drag-to-aim provides intuitive analog control over a physics system.

prototypes.name: summer-games
prototypes.mechanic: alternate-mash and timed-press across four athletic events
prototypes.ancestry: Summer Games (1984, Epyx), Track & Field (1983, Konami)
prototypes.controls: ← → — alternate mash for speed | Space — jump/throw/action | Enter — next event | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — multi-modal game design, varied control schemes)
prototypes.aiInsight: Each event is a different optimization problem sharing the same interface — the player must rapidly switch mental models between speed-maximization (mashing), timing-optimization (jumping), and angle-selection (throwing). This multi-task learning from a unified input space prefigures transfer learning across related domains.
prototypes.notes: Button mashing as speed input is deliberately physical.

prototypes.name: skateboard
prototypes.mechanic: pump halfpipe, launch off lip, perform tricks for combo score
prototypes.ancestry: Skate or Die (1987, Electronic Arts), 720° (1986, Atari Games)
prototypes.controls: ← → — pump/move | Space — launch off lip | Z X C — tricks | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — physics engines, animation state machines)
prototypes.aiInsight: The halfpipe creates a natural oscillator — the player's skill is in timing inputs to amplify the oscillation (pumping) and then exploiting peak amplitude (air time) to perform actions. This resonance-exploitation pattern appears in control theory as energy pumping in nonlinear oscillators.
prototypes.notes: Pumping the halfpipe is the hidden skill — timing builds amplitude.

prototypes.name: slalom
prototypes.mechanic: carve downhill through gates, avoid obstacles, beat the clock
prototypes.ancestry: Slalom (1987, Rare/Nintendo), Ski Free (1991, Microsoft/Chris Pirih)
prototypes.controls: <- -> — carve/turn | up — tuck for speed | down — brake/snowplow | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — pseudo-3D rendering, physics simulation)
prototypes.aiInsight: Slalom skiing is a sequential decision problem under momentum constraints — each gate approach must account for current velocity and the position of the NEXT gate. The player is solving a look-ahead path optimization where greedy choices (shortest path to current gate) may make the next gate unreachable.
prototypes.notes: Speed-turn tradeoff is the core tension — faster means wider turns.

prototypes.name: slope-run
prototypes.mechanic: downhill freestyle skiing — dodge obstacles, perform jumps, outrun the yeti
prototypes.ancestry: SkiFree (1991, Chris Pirih, Microsoft), Skiing (1980, Activision)
prototypes.controls: Left/Right — steer | Down — speed boost | Up — slow down | Space — jump | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 4 (Statistical Learning — procedural obstacle generation)
prototypes.aiInsight: SkiFree's infamous Abominable Snowman is a chase agent with a simple but effective catch-up algorithm — it always runs faster than the player, making escape feel impossible but creating a memorable emergent narrative. The yeti became a cultural touchstone for unexpected game AI behavior.
prototypes.notes: Downhill skiing. Dodge trees, rocks, other skiers. Jump off ramps.

prototypes.name: carsoccer
prototypes.mechanic: drive car to hit ball into goal with boost physics
prototypes.ancestry: Rocket League (2015, Psyonix), Soccar/SARPBC (2008, Psyonix), Micro Machines (1991, Codemasters)
prototypes.controls: Up/Down — accelerate/brake | Left/Right — steer | Shift — boost | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 6 (Transformer Era — physics prediction, trajectory planning)
prototypes.aiInsight: Rocket League's genius was combining two solved problems — driving physics and ball physics — into an emergent whole greater than either part. The car-ball interaction creates a control problem so rich that even deep RL agents struggle to master aerial play.
prototypes.notes: Car soccer distills vehicular sports to pure physics interaction.

prototypes.name: darts
prototypes.mechanic: precision aiming — aim and throw darts at a scored dartboard; 501 countdown with doubles to finish
prototypes.ancestry: Medieval arrow throwing (~1860s England), Phil Taylor era, PDC
prototypes.controls: Mouse — aim | Click+hold — set power | Release — throw | R — new game | ? — info
