prototypes.aiEra: 5 (Statistical Learning — motor control optimization, precision robotics)
prototypes.aiInsight: Expert darts players exhibit motor precision under pressure. The optimal target depends on your accuracy: beginners should aim for the large 19/7 region, not triple-20. This is the explore-exploit tradeoff — play safe or gamble for high scores. The Kelly criterion from gambling theory applies directly.
prototypes.notes: Darts evolved from soldiers throwing shortened arrows at tree-trunk cross-sections. The modern numbering (invented by Brian Gamlin, 1896) deliberately places low numbers next to high ones — punishing inaccuracy. Professional level: 100+ average per 3-dart visit.

prototypes.name: bowling
prototypes.mechanic: lane physics — aim, set power and spin, roll a ball down a lane to knock down 10 pins; spare and strike scoring across 10 frames
prototypes.ancestry: Ancient Egypt (~3200 BCE), Nine-pin (~1300s Germany), Ten-pin (~1840s New York)
prototypes.controls: Left/Right — aim | Space — set power (press), release to throw | A/D — add spin | R — new game | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — physics simulation, trajectory optimization)
prototypes.aiInsight: Pin scatter is a chain-reaction physics problem: the ball hits the lead pin, which hits neighboring pins, which hit further pins. A perfect strike requires hitting the 1-3 pocket at the correct angle. The pin action is deterministic physics that feels chaotic — the same seeming randomness that emerges from simple physical rules.
prototypes.notes: Bowling pins were found in an Egyptian tomb dating to 3200 BCE. The modern 10-pin triangle formation (1841, New York) and standardized lane dimensions create a precision challenge. The scoring system (spares add next throw, strikes add next two) is elegant game design.

prototypes.name: ragdoll-run
prototypes.mechanic: awkward body physics — Q/W control thighs, O/P control calves; goal is to run 100 meters but most players can't get past 2 meters
prototypes.ancestry: QWOP (2008, Bennett Foddy), GIRP (2011, Bennett Foddy), Getting Over It (2017, Bennett Foddy)
prototypes.controls: Q — left thigh fwd / right thigh back | W — right thigh fwd / left thigh back | O — left calf fwd | P — right calf back | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 5-6 (Expert Systems to Transformer Era — deliberate difficulty through unintuitive controls; RL agents struggle with coordination)
prototypes.aiInsight: QWOP is a masterclass in difficulty through interface design rather than content. The game is trivially simple in concept but nearly impossible in execution because the control mapping violates all motor learning intuitions. RL agents can eventually solve it but require millions of episodes — mirroring how humans must build entirely new neural pathways.
prototypes.notes: Bennett Foddy's genius was recognizing that the gap between intention and execution IS the game.

prototypes.name: carrom
prototypes.mechanic: disc-flicking precision — flick a striker disc to pocket carrom men into corner pockets; sink your 9 pieces + the queen before opponent
prototypes.ancestry: Carrom (~18th century, South Asia), Crokinole (1876, Canada), Pitchnut, Novuss (Latvia)
prototypes.controls: Click+Drag — aim and set power | Release — flick striker | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Expert Systems — angle calculation, trajectory prediction)
prototypes.aiInsight: Carrom's physics puzzle is deceptively complex: multi-disc collisions create chaotic outcomes from precise inputs. An expert system can calculate ideal angles and power for single-shot pocketing, but planning multi-bounce combination shots requires search through continuous state spaces.
prototypes.notes: Traditional South Asian board game dating to ~18th century India.

prototypes.name: petanque
prototypes.mechanic: precision ball placement — throw metal boules to land closest to a small wooden cochonnet (jack); can knock opponent's boules away; point = gentle placement, shoot = knock away
prototypes.ancestry: Jeu provençal (France, ancient), Pétanque (1907, La Ciotat, Jules Lenoir), Bocce (Italy)
prototypes.controls: Mouse — aim direction | Click+drag — set power & angle | Release — throw | R — new round | N — new game | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Expert Systems — traditional Provençal, 1907 modern rules)
prototypes.aiInsight: Pétanque strategy is a classic example of the point-vs-shoot decision — a minimax problem where players choose between safe placement (pointing) and risky displacement (shooting). The optimal strategy depends on the current score differential and remaining boules, creating a dynamic programming problem similar to poker pot odds.
prototypes.notes: Pétanque originated in La Ciotat, France in 1907 when Jules Lenoir, unable to run due to rheumatism, modified jeu provençal to be played standing still (pieds tanqués = feet planted). Players throw metal boules toward a small wooden target ball called the cochonnet (piglet). The game spread globally and is now played in 160+ countries.

prototypes.name: sepaktakraw
prototypes.mechanic: kick volleyball — hit a rattan ball over a net using feet, knees, chest, head; no hands allowed; acrobatic bicycle kicks for spikes
prototypes.ancestry: Sepak Takraw (traditional, ~15th century Southeast Asia), Chinlone (Myanmar), Sipa (Philippines), Takraw (Thailand)
prototypes.controls: Left/Right — move | Space — kick ball | Up+Space — high kick | Down+Space — low kick | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Expert Systems — rule-based opponent positioning and shot selection)
prototypes.aiInsight: Sepak takraw's acrobatic nature makes AI control fascinating — the bicycle kick requires precise timing of jump, rotation, and contact. Early sports game AI used simple heuristics for positioning; this game's AI mirrors that approach with rule-based court coverage and shot timing.
prototypes.notes: Traditional Southeast Asian sport dating to 15th century Malacca Sultanate.

prototypes.name: kabaddi
prototypes.mechanic: raid and return — one raider enters enemy half, must tag opponents and return in one breath (timer); defenders try to tackle and hold the raider
prototypes.ancestry: Kabaddi (~2000 BCE, Indian subcontinent), Pro Kabaddi League (2014, Star Sports)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move | Space — tackle (defend mode) | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Expert Systems — rule-based pursuit/evasion, zone control)
prototypes.aiInsight: Kabaddi's raid mechanic is a natural pursuit-evasion problem. Defenders must coordinate zone coverage while the raider optimizes a path that maximizes tags under a strict time constraint — a variant of the travelling salesman problem with adversarial obstacles and a hard deadline.
prototypes.notes: Raid-and-return creates unique risk-reward tension unlike any Western sport.

prototypes.name: nanacacrash
prototypes.mechanic: launch and bounce — crash a bicycle into a person, launching them; they bounce off characters in the field who boost or block; maximize distance through chain bounces
prototypes.ancestry: Nanaca Crash (2005, Anonymous Japanese Flash developer), Yeti Sports (2004, Chris Hilgert), Kitten Cannon (2005, Dan Fleming)
prototypes.controls: Click — set angle / set power / aerial boost | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 5 (Expert Systems — optimization of launch parameters, trajectory prediction, character placement RNG)
prototypes.aiInsight: Nanaca Crash went viral in Japan as a Flash game in 2005, pioneering the launch-and-bounce genre. The deceptive simplicity — two clicks to launch, then pray for good bounces — hides a genuine optimization problem: angle and power selection interact nonlinearly with random character placement to create enormous variance in outcomes.
prototypes.notes: The charm is in the absurd ragdoll physics and chain bounces.

prototypes.name: ddakji
prototypes.mechanic: paper card slamming — slam your folded paper card onto opponent's card to flip it over; angle and power determine success; physics of card flipping
prototypes.ancestry: Ddakji (딱지, traditional Korean children's street game), Menko (Japan), Pog (1990s, Hawaii)
prototypes.controls: Click+Drag — set angle and power | Release — slam card | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Expert Systems — angle/power optimization for flip probability)
prototypes.aiInsight: Ddakji's flip mechanic is a fascinating physics puzzle where air displacement and impact force combine. The optimal slam angle creates maximum air pressure under the target card's edge. An expert system can model the probability curve but the real-world chaos of paper aerodynamics makes perfect play impossible — a satisfying blend of skill and chance.
prototypes.notes: Traditional Korean children's street game, made globally famous by Squid Game (2021).

prototypes.name: gonggi
prototypes.mechanic: Korean jacks — toss one stone up, pick up stones from the ground, catch the tossed stone before it lands; progressive rounds increase difficulty (pick 1, then 2, then 3, then 4, then all 5)
prototypes.ancestry: Gonggi (공기놀이, Korea, centuries-old), Knucklebones (ancient Greece/Rome), Jacks (worldwide variants), Otedama (Japan)
prototypes.controls: Click stone — toss it up | Click ground stones — pick up while tossed stone is airborne | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 2 (Heuristic — timing windows, pickup ordering)
prototypes.aiInsight: Gonggi's core challenge is a real-time dexterity test with sequential constraints. The timing window between toss and catch creates a natural difficulty curve as more stones must be gathered in each round. An AI needs only simple timing heuristics — the human challenge is entirely in motor coordination.
prototypes.notes: Traditional Korean stone-tossing game played for centuries.

prototypes.name: ssireum
prototypes.mechanic: Korean wrestling — two wrestlers grip each other's satba waist band; push, pull, and trip; first to make opponent's body above the knee touch the sandy ground wins
prototypes.ancestry: Ssireum (Korean traditional wrestling, UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage, depicted in 4th-century Goguryeo tomb murals at Anak and Jiangchuan), Sumo (Japanese wrestling), Bökh (Mongolian wrestling)
prototypes.controls: Left/Right — push/pull (shift balance) | Up — lift attempt | Down — trip attempt | Space — power burst | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — FSM opponent with telegraphed techniques, balance-based win condition)
prototypes.aiInsight: Ssireum reduces to a balance prediction problem. Each wrestler is a dynamic system with a center of gravity — techniques shift this balance, and the opponent must counter-predict. The AI opponent uses a simple FSM with weighted random technique selection biased by its own balance state, mirroring early game AI heuristics.
prototypes.notes: Ssireum (씨름) is Korea's oldest sport, depicted in Goguryeo tomb murals from the 4th century CE.

prototypes.name: tuho
prototypes.mechanic: arrow throwing precision — throw thin arrows into a narrow-necked pot from a distance; arcs follow physics; score based on arrows that land inside
prototypes.ancestry: Tuho (투호, Korea, ~5th century), Touhu (投壺, China, ~5th century BCE), Toko (投壺, Japan), Korean aristocratic court game
prototypes.controls: Click+drag — set angle and power | Release — throw | R — new game | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 2 (Expert Systems — trajectory calculation, ballistic prediction)
prototypes.aiInsight: Tuho is a deceptively simple ballistic problem. The narrow pot opening creates a tight constraint on the angle-velocity space. An expert system can solve the optimal throw analytically, but human players must develop muscle memory through repetition — the same motor learning challenge that makes darts and archery compelling.
prototypes.notes: Tuho (투호) is a traditional Korean arrow-throwing game played by aristocrats during the Joseon dynasty.

prototypes.name: jegichagi
prototypes.mechanic: keepie uppie — kick a jegi (weighted shuttlecock) to keep it in the air using only your foot; time kicks to maintain height; consecutive kicks build combo score
prototypes.ancestry: Jegichagi (traditional Korean, centuries old), Hacky Sack / Footbag (1972, John Stalberger), Keepy Uppy / Kickups (universal folk game)
prototypes.controls: Left/Right — move foot | Space/Click — kick | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 2 (traditional Korean foot game, 제기차기, played during Lunar New Year)
prototypes.aiInsight: Jegichagi is a timing-purity game — the entire challenge reduces to a single well-timed input repeated with increasing precision. The rhythm of kick-wait-kick creates a meditative flow state similar to juggling or yo-yo play, where the player internalizes gravity as muscle memory.
prototypes.notes: Korean traditional game played especially during Seollal (Lunar New Year).

prototypes.name: paengi
prototypes.mechanic: top spinning — spin a top and keep it spinning by whipping it at the right moment; the top slows from friction; time your whip strikes to maintain spin speed
prototypes.ancestry: Paengi (팽이, Korea), Beyblade (2000, Japan), Dreidel (Hanukkah, Jewish), Trompo (Latin America), Lattu (South Asia)
prototypes.controls: Space/Click — whip the top | Left/Right — reposition stance | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 2 (Heuristic Bots — timing windows, friction model)
prototypes.aiInsight: A top-spinning survival game reduces to a timing optimization problem. The ideal whip moment depends on current RPM and friction curve — a heuristic bot can learn the optimal strike window. Wind adds a spatial dimension requiring positional adjustment.
prototypes.notes: Traditional Korean winter game (팽이치기) played on frozen ground.

prototypes.name: golf-aim
prototypes.mechanic: anime golf — power bar click for distance, timing click for accuracy, spin control; pangya shot for perfect timing bonus; fantasy courses
prototypes.ancestry: Pangya (2004, Ntreev Soft, Korea), Hot Shots Golf (1997, Clap Hanz), Mario Golf (1999, Camelot)
prototypes.controls: Left/Right — aim | Space — start power bar / set power / set accuracy | Up/Down — adjust spin | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 5 (Deep Learning — online multiplayer matchmaking and progression systems)
prototypes.aiInsight: Pangya's three-click swing mechanic decomposes the continuous challenge of hitting a golf ball into three discrete timing decisions. Each click is a separate test of motor skill, turning a smooth analog action into a rhythm game. The 'Pangya!' perfect shot — requiring all three clicks in ideal zones — creates a Skinnerian variable-ratio reward schedule that drove Korea's PC bang addiction to the game.
prototypes.notes: Korean anime golf (2004). Three-click mechanic: power, accuracy, spin.

prototypes.name: rhythm-dance
prototypes.mechanic: dance key combo — arrow key sequences scroll as combos; input the full combo then press Space on the beat; more complex combos = more impressive dance moves
prototypes.ancestry: Audition Online (2004, T3 Entertainment), Pump It Up (1999, Andamiro), Dance Dance Revolution (1998, Konami), BeatMania (1997, Konami)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — input combo sequence | Space — confirm on the beat | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 5 (Statistical Learning — sequence memorization, temporal beat alignment)
prototypes.aiInsight: Audition Online separates the rhythm game into two cognitive phases: first memorize a sequence (working memory), then execute with precise timing (motor timing). This two-phase structure mirrors how statistical models handle sequence-to-sequence tasks — encode the input, then decode at the right moment. The combo length scaling tests working memory capacity, which in humans is famously limited to 7±2 items.
prototypes.notes: T3 Entertainment's Audition Online (2004) became Korea's most popular casual online game, spawning versions across Southeast Asia and Latin America.

prototypes.name: freestyle
prototypes.mechanic: street basketball — 1v1 half-court basketball; timed button presses for dribble moves, shots, and blocks; position-based play
prototypes.ancestry: Freestyle (2005, JCE, Korea), NBA Jam (1993, Midway), NBA Street (2001, EA)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move | Space — shoot (hold for power) | Z — pass/steal | X — special dribble move | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 5 (Deep Learning — adaptive AI defender reads player patterns)
prototypes.aiInsight: Freestyle's AI opponents used pattern recognition to predict player tendencies — if you always drove left, the AI would shade that direction. This created a rock-paper-scissors metagame where players had to vary their approach, mirroring how real basketball defenders study film.
prototypes.notes: Korean street basketball 1v1. Timed shooting mechanic (release at jump peak).

prototypes.name: dance-arrows
prototypes.mechanic: 5-panel rhythm — adds diagonal arrows (upper-left, upper-right) plus a center panel to DDR's directional concept; creates completely different step patterns; Korea's answer to DDR
prototypes.ancestry: Pump It Up (1999, Andamiro/Korea), Dance Dance Revolution (1998, Konami), PaRappa the Rapper (1996, NanaOn-Sha)
prototypes.controls: Q — upper-left | Z — lower-left | Space — center | M — lower-right | P — upper-right | Enter — start/select | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 5 (Statistical Learning — temporal pattern matching, sequence prediction for rhythm and step timing)
prototypes.aiInsight: Pump It Up's 5-panel diamond layout creates a fundamentally different combinatorial space than DDR's 4-panel cross. The center panel enables crossover patterns and spins impossible on 4 panels. Step chart design is a constraint satisfaction problem — patterns must be physically executable while maximizing musical expression.
prototypes.notes: Andamiro's Pump It Up (펌프 잇 업, 1999) was Korea's original dance game.

prototypes.name: rhythm-keys
prototypes.mechanic: multi-lane premium rhythm — notes fall in 6 lanes; hit corresponding keys with precise timing; long notes require holding; premium feel with music visualization
prototypes.ancestry: DJMAX (2004, Pentavision/Korea), Beatmania IIDX (1999, Konami), EZ2DJ (1999, Amuseworld), O2Jam (2002, O2Media)
prototypes.controls: S/D/F/J/K/L — hit lanes 1-6 | Space — start/pause | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 6 (Transformer Era — multi-lane pattern recognition, temporal sequence prediction)
prototypes.aiInsight: DJMAX's 6-lane layout forces bimanual coordination — left hand on S/D/F, right hand on J/K/L — creating a split-attention problem analogous to multi-head attention in transformers. The long notes add a state-tracking dimension: the model must maintain which keys are currently held while processing incoming notes.
prototypes.notes: DJMAX (2004) by Pentavision defined Korean premium rhythm gaming.

prototypes.name: bar-serve
prototypes.mechanic: serve beer along multi-lane bar, catch returning mugs before they fall off
prototypes.ancestry: Tapper (1983), Root Beer Tapper (1983), Diner Dash (2004)
prototypes.controls: Up/Down arrows or W/S — switch lane | Space or Enter — serve | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — rule-based dispatch, pre-neural)
prototypes.aiInsight: Tapper is a real-time multi-queue scheduling problem identical to CPU task dispatch, elevator routing, and later RL environments. The player IS the scheduler — choosing between N queues with different urgency levels and no lookahead. John Miller's bartender intuition ran on the same algorithm as Dijkstra's scheduler.
prototypes.notes: Four simultaneous queues with stochastic arrival rates. Core mechanic demands priority arbitration under incomplete information.

prototypes.name: food-stack
prototypes.mechanic: walk over burger parts to drop them, assembling burgers while avoiding chasers
prototypes.ancestry: BurgerTime (1982, Data East), Peter Pepper's Ice Cream Factory (1984, Data East)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys — move | Space — pepper spray | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — deterministic chase AI with patrol paths)
prototypes.aiInsight: BurgerTime's enemies use simple pursuit algorithms but the multi-level platform structure creates emergent complexity — the player must plan paths that manipulate enemy positions while completing the burger assembly objective.
prototypes.notes: Walk over burger ingredients (bun, lettuce, meat, bun) to drop them down.

prototypes.name: delivery-run
prototypes.mechanic: deliver papers to houses while dodging obstacles on bicycle
prototypes.ancestry: Paperboy (1985, Atari Games)
prototypes.controls: ↑ ↓ — change lane | ← → — speed | Space — throw paper | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — multi-objective gameplay, procedural obstacle placement)
prototypes.aiInsight: Paperboy is a multi-objective optimization problem — the player simultaneously maximizes deliveries (accuracy), minimizes damage (avoidance), and manages limited resources (papers). The subscription mechanic adds a meta-game where current performance affects future difficulty, creating a feedback loop between skill and challenge.
prototypes.notes: Delivering papers IS the game — the obstacles are secondary to the service.

prototypes.name: kitchen
prototypes.mechanic: kitchen service — grab ingredients, cook dishes in sequence, plate and serve orders before they expire; manage multiple orders under time pressure
prototypes.ancestry: BurgerTime (1982), Cooking Mama (2006), Overcooked (2016), PlateUp! (2022)
prototypes.controls: Arrow keys or WASD — move | Space — pick up/put down/interact | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 6 (Deep Learning — task scheduling, resource allocation)
prototypes.aiInsight: Kitchen service is a real-time scheduling problem with resource constraints: multiple orders compete for limited cooking stations, ingredients, and player time. The player must solve a dynamic job-shop scheduling problem — the same NP-hard optimization problem that factory automation and logistics AI tackles. Overcooked proved that scheduling stress, when framed as cooperative chaos, is inherently fun.
prototypes.notes: Overcooked (2016) by Ghost Town Games turned cooking into controlled chaos. The genre descends from BurgerTime (1982) and Tapper (1983) but adds spatial movement and multi-step recipes. The cognitive load of tracking multiple timers and order priorities creates satisfying flow state.

prototypes.name: split-bounce
prototypes.mechanic: territory division — build walls to divide the playing field and trap bouncing balls; claim 75% of the area to clear each level
prototypes.ancestry: Qix (1981), JezzBall (1992 Microsoft), Barrack (1998), Volfied (1989)
prototypes.controls: Click — start building wall | Tab — toggle wall direction (H/V) | R — restart | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 3 (Knowledge Explosion — spatial partitioning, computational geometry)
prototypes.aiInsight: JezzBall is a real-time spatial partitioning problem. Each wall divides the space into regions that must be evaluated for ball containment. The optimal strategy requires reasoning about area maximization under dynamic constraints — the same computational geometry problems that spatial indexing (BSP trees, quadtrees) solves. The player performs real-time geometric reasoning that maps directly to algorithms developed for computer graphics.
prototypes.notes: JezzBall (1992) was included in Microsoft Entertainment Pack 3. It simplifies Qix's freeform line-drawing into axis-aligned walls, making the spatial reasoning more accessible while preserving the core territory-capture tension. The key insight: you're not fighting the balls, you're partitioning space around them.

prototypes.name: linerider
prototypes.mechanic: physics sandbox drawing — draw lines on the canvas, then press play to watch a tiny sledder ride them following physics; no objectives, pure creative toy
prototypes.ancestry: Line Rider (2006, Boštjan Čadež), Crayon Physics (2007, Petri Purho)
prototypes.controls: Mouse — draw/erase lines | Space — toggle draw/play | E — eraser tool | P — pencil tool | C — clear all | R — restart sledder | ? — info
prototypes.aiEra: 5 (Deep Learning Dawn — GPU acceleration, early neural nets)
prototypes.aiInsight: Line Rider is a physics sandbox where the player IS the level designer. The core loop — draw, simulate, observe, iterate — mirrors the scientific method. AI could optimize tracks but would miss the creative joy of experimentation.
prototypes.notes: Pure creative toy with no win/lose state. The satisfaction comes from watching physics play out on your hand-drawn track.
